Sources section cites at least two external works.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-17T22:37:54Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1512290","created_at":"2026-04-17T22:10:38.363323602Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-13ff86c6","depends_on_id":"ddx-5241bb19","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-17T22:12:23Z"}],"description":"The existing library/personas/test-engineer-tdd.md is the strongest shipped persona content-wise, but it references DDx-internal paths that only exist in the DDx repo (docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md, cli/internal/agent/script.go, named DDx legacy offenders like orchTestGitOps/fakeExecuteBeadGit/gateTestGitOps/mockExecutor).

Rename to library/personas/test-engineer.md and generalize.

**CORE STANCES (load-bearing — preserve these):**
- Favor real e2e tests. At the e2e layer, do not mock anything — real infrastructure, real processes, real network where applicable. E2e tests that mock the database or the HTTP layer are unit tests lying about their scope.
- Stubs over mocks at the unit layer. Extract interfaces at domain boundaries; write in-memory stubs that return canned values set up by the test. Stubs do not record call sequences.
- Contract tests between in-memory stubs and real backends — same suite runs against both, proving they are interchangeable. This is the discipline that keeps stubs honest.
- Zero mocks in integration tests. Use real git (temp dir with scrubbed env), real filesystem (t.TempDir), real database (temp file / temp container), real HTTP (local test server). The only approved fake is at the AI-provider (or equivalent third-party-SDK) boundary.
- Behavior over implementation — assert on observable state, not on call counts or invocation sequences.
- Deterministic factory functions / fakers over static JSON fixtures.
- Test names describe behavior ("creates invoice when all line items valid"), not positions ("test_create_invoice_3").
- Testing pyramid as a *shape guide* (more unit than integration than e2e), not as mandated ratios.
- **Performance measurements require a baseline and clear boundaries.** Any claim that something is "fast", "scales", or "improved performance" must include: (a) a numeric baseline to compare against (prior version, prior run, published SLO), (b) an explicit boundary — what unit of work is being measured, what environment, what is excluded (cold-start, warmup, network latency, etc.), (c) reproducible harness so the measurement can be re-run. "It feels fast" and "benchmarks look good" are not performance claims.

**STRIP (cargo-cult testing dogma — do NOT ship):**
- Hard coverage percentages as universal rules (">= 80% overall", "100% on critical paths"). Replace with: "measure coverage where the project tracks it; coverage is a signal, not a target".
- Specific timing budgets as universal rules ("unit < 10ms", "integration < 100ms"). Replace with: "tests should be fast enough that developers run them; the specific threshold is a project concern".
- Specific pyramid percentages ("60% unit / 25% integration / 10% contract / 5% e2e") — keep the shape, drop the numbers.
- TDD as the only valid approach. Test-first is one workflow; test-alongside is another; test-after for a specific reason can be defensible. Do not call non-TDD "worst practice".
- Tests-for-the-sake-of-tests (mirror-of-spec tests that add no signal, trivial getters/setters).
- Mutation testing as a quality requirement. Mention as an optional signal, not a bar to clear.
- Snapshot tests without a maintenance plan.
- Retry loops around flaky tests as "improvement" — a retry loop hides a bug; fix the flake.
- Branch coverage vs line coverage dogma — say "coverage metrics matter less than whether tests catch real bugs".

**STRIP (DDx-internal specifics):**
- References to cli/internal/agent/script.go (replace with generic "a deterministic script-like fake at the AI-provider boundary")
- Named DDx legacy offenders (orchTestGitOps, fakeExecuteBeadGit, gateTestGitOps, mockExecutor) — these belong in a helix-test-engineer plugin persona, not this generic base
- Authoritative-sources frontmatter section citing docs/helix/concerns.md and HELIX concern.md
- Specific paths like cli/internal/bead, cli/internal/agent

Ground in external canon:
- Kent Beck - Test-Driven Development: By Example
- Martin Fowler - "Mocks Arent Stubs" (canonical reference for why call-sequence assertions are a code smell)
- Brendan Gregg - Systems Performance methodology (for the baseline + boundary stance on perf measurement)
- Anthropic Prompt Library (if a testing entry exists)
- TDD Guard from anthropics/skills repo (if the automated-test-first-enforcement pattern is worth mentioning)

Body structure: Philosophy / Approach / Anti-patterns / Sources.

The existing file is a textbook summary of C4, ADR, microservices, CQRS, SAGA, event sourcing — a laundry list of patterns without opinionated voice. Rewrite with real stances.

Core stances:
- Simplicity beats sophistication — the right architecture is the simplest one that works for the real load, not the one that scales to 10x imagined load
- Prefer monolith first — microservices have operational cost that only pays back at real scale
- Event-driven only when async decoupling earns it — not by default
- Data model beats clever caching — design the schema right, the cache falls out naturally
- Write ADRs for decisions that will bite future maintainers; don't write ADRs for decisions with no real alternatives
- Most 'architecture' failures are requirements failures or team-structure failures in disguise

Body structure: Philosophy / Approach / Anti-patterns / Sources.

Anti-patterns:
- Designing for 10x scale before validating 1x
- Microservices because 'teams should own services' without validated team boundaries
- Event sourcing for audit because 'it's clean' — audit logs solve audit without event sourcing's operational tax
- Abstracting over unclear requirements (YAGNI at the architecture layer)
- Reaching for a pattern name before understanding the actual constraint

Sources:
- Anthropic Prompt Library architecture prompts (if any)
- Gregor Hohpe's architecture writings
- Stefan Tilkov's monolith/microservices talks
- Martin Fowler 'Monolith First' and similar
- Reference to the C4 model (Simon Brown) and ADR (Michael Nygard) as conventions for output, not dogma

The existing file has a generic tutorial tone; rewrite with real opinions.

Core stances:
- YAGNI — implement only what is specified; no speculative features or "while we are here" additions
- KISS — simplest solution that meets requirements; avoid clever code and premature optimization
- DOWITYTD (Do Only What I Told You To Do) — stop when the task is complete
- Ship tests with code — implementations without a test command referenced in AC are half-done (but: test-first vs test-alongside vs test-after is a workflow choice, not mandated by this persona — see the test-engineer persona for the testing stance; this persona requires tests exist and pass, not a specific methodology)
- Do not design for hypothetical future requirements. Three similar lines is better than a premature abstraction.
- **Performance claims require baselines.** Do not add code "for performance" without a baseline measurement and a clear boundary describing what is being measured. "This is faster" without a number is not a justification. (Cross-reference test-engineer for the measurement discipline.)
- **Favor real e2e validation at the integration boundary.** When in doubt between a layer of abstraction/mocking and writing a real integration test, write the real integration test. The cost of mocks compounds; the cost of a real e2e test is paid once.

Body structure: Philosophy / Approach / Anti-patterns / Sources.

Anti-patterns to call out:
- Speculative abstraction (base classes, interfaces, config knobs for "someday")
- Refactoring unrelated code during a bug fix ("while we are here...")
- Adding error handling for scenarios that cannot happen (trust internal invariants; validate at system boundaries)
- Leaving TODO comments for problems that should block the current change
- Comments that restate what the code does (well-named identifiers already do that)
- Adding a mock or stub when a real integration test would be simpler and catch more bugs
- Making performance claims without baselines or boundaries

Sources:
- YAGNI principle (c2.com wiki, various Martin Fowler articles)
- Anthropic Prompt Library entries for coding agents, if any fit
- Relevant DDx system-prompt content from library/prompts/claude/system-prompts/focused.md (already documents YAGNI/KISS/DOWITYTD)
- Brendan Gregg on performance measurement discipline (baseline + boundary)

---

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:523:{"acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer looking at the personas UI, I want to understand at a glance what personas are, where they come from (library vs project), and I want to fork or create a project-local persona without leaving the UI. Library content stays read-only so I cannot accidentally mutate shared content; project content is fully editable.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Design note + policy decision recorded.** Before implementation, a one-page note in `docs/helix/02-design/` captures D1 (library vs project policy, override rules, file locations) and D2 (scoped UI additions). Implementation PR links to it.

2. **Project-local persona directory.** `.ddx/personas/*.md` is a recognized location. `ddx persona --list` shows both library and project personas with a `source` tag. Override rule: a project persona with the same name as a library persona wins for that project's bindings; unit test covers this precedence.

3. **GraphQL additions.** New mutations: `personaCreate(name, body, projectId) → Persona`, `personaUpdate(name, body, projectId) → Persona`, `personaDelete(name, projectId) → {ok}`. All gated on `source == "project"`; attempting any of them on a library persona returns a typed error. Each mutation persists to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` under the given project.

4. **CLI parity.** `ddx persona new`, `edit`, `fork`, `delete` exist and share the same underlying operations as the GraphQL mutations (no duplicated logic). Integration test asserts that a `ddx persona new` followed by a GraphQL `personas` query returns the new persona.

5. **UI — explainer copy.** The personas page shows a one-sentence description of what personas are and a second sentence distinguishing library vs project sources. Playwright asserts both sentences are present.

6. **UI — source badge.** Each persona row shows a `library` or `project` badge. Playwright seeds one of each and asserts correct badges.

7. **UI — New / Edit / Delete for project personas.** A "New persona" button opens a form with name + body fields. Project-source rows have Edit and Delete affordances. Library-source rows have neither. Playwright creates a persona, edits it, verifies the edit persisted, then deletes it.

8. **UI — Fork library to project.** Library rows have a "Fork to project" action. Clicking it prompts for a name (default: library name with `-local` suffix on collision), creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md`, and navigates to the editor. Playwright asserts the forked file exists and opens in the editor.

9. **No regressions.** Existing list/show/bind flows still work. Playwright smoke of the pre-existing binding UX passes.

10. **Empty project-local state.** When a project has zero project-local personas, the UI shows a subtle hint: "No project personas yet. Fork a library persona or create a new one." Playwright asserts.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"69c60d902a41f3a24af908881f4ef14ba1ff3832","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:30:40.66472156Z","description":"## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../personas` is a well-crafted browser + bind UX, but it is read-only: no add, no edit, no delete, no explanation of what the reader is looking at or why they cannot change it. A user looking at it can reasonably ask "what is this for?" and "why can't I change anything?" and the UI answers neither.

## Ground truth

- **What a persona is.** A markdown file with frontmatter under `library/personas/*.md` defining AI personality, expertise, principles, communication style. Shipped defaults: architect, code-reviewer, implementer, specification-enforcer, test-engineer.
- **How it's used.** Projects declare role→persona bindings in `.ddx/config.yaml` via `persona_bindings`. When an agent performs role X, the bound persona's body is injected into its system prompt.
- **What the tool exposes today.**
  - CLI: `ddx persona --list`, `--show <name>`, `--bind <persona> --role <role>`. No create/edit/delete.
  - GraphQL: `personaBind` mutation only (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:150`). No `personaCreate/Update/Delete` in schema.
  - UI: list/show/bind parity with CLI.
- **Why it evolved this way.** Personas have been treated as *library content* — authored carefully, version-controlled upstream, pulled into projects via `ddx update` and contributed back via `ddx contribute`. The tool deliberately did not become a persona editor.

## The gap

The library-only model doesn't hold up for real use:

1. **Project-local personas are a real need.** Teams want "our code-reviewer, with our opinions about our codebase." Today they either (a) fork the persona file and push upstream (wrong — it's not generally useful), (b) never customize (bad outcomes), or (c) drop a file into some ad-hoc local path and hope it works.
2. **The UI implies capability it doesn't have.** A list + detail view with no hint about source policy reads as "unfinished feature" rather than "intentional read-only".
3. **Binding alone isn't enough.** Even if editing stays out of scope, users need to understand *which personas are available*, *where they come from* (library vs project-local), and *how to change that*.

## Proposed direction

Two decisions (pick before implementation):

### D1 — Policy: who owns persona content?

Recommend: **both, with a clear boundary.**
- Library personas: `library/personas/*.md` — read-only in UI, contribute upstream via `ddx contribute`.
- Project-local personas: new directory `.ddx/personas/*.md` — fully editable in UI, live with the project, not in the shared library. Override semantics: a project-local persona with the same name as a library persona takes precedence for that project's bindings.

### D2 — Scope of the UI

Minimum viable, given D1:
- **Explainer copy** on the personas page header: "Personas are AI personality templates. Library personas are shared; project personas live with this project. Bind a persona to a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`."
- **Source badge** on each persona row: `library` or `project`.
- **Project-local editor.** For project personas:
  - `New persona` button → form (name, roles/tags, body markdown). Creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` with frontmatter.
  - `Edit` on project rows.
  - `Delete` on project rows with confirm.
- **Library rows stay read-only.** Action on a library row: `Fork to project` — copies the library file to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` (with a default rename suggestion on collision) and opens the editor. No in-place edit, no delete.
- **Bind affordance remains.** Binding UI continues to work for both sources.

## What this bead does NOT decide

- Multi-project persona sharing (e.g., org-level personas between library and project). File a follow-up if it becomes pressing.
- Rich editor affordances beyond a plain markdown textarea with preview. Frontmatter can be a YAML-ish textarea for v1.
- Syncing project personas upstream — that remains `ddx contribute` and is out of scope.
- Any change to how personas are injected into agent prompts (the read path on the server).

## CLI parity

Whatever the UI gains, the CLI gains too. Add:
- `ddx persona new <name>` — creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` scaffold.
- `ddx persona edit <name>` — opens `$EDITOR` on the project-local persona (errors on library).
- `ddx persona fork <library-name> [--as <new-name>]` — copies library → project.
- `ddx persona delete <name>` — project-local only.

UI and CLI call the same underlying `cli/internal/persona` operations; no duplicate logic.","events_attachment":"ddx-9e0eff22/events.jsonl","execute-loop-last-detail":"escalation exhausted: unsupported model "minimax/minimax-m2.7" for harness "gemini"; supported models: gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","execute-loop-no-changes-count":1,"feature":"FEAT-008","id":"ddx-9e0eff22","issue_type":"task","labels":["feat-008","personas","ui","design"],"notes":"REVIEW:BLOCK

Rewrite it as a first-class persona because spec-driven development is a first-class DDx concern: beads link to spec-id; the governing-artifact model depends on someone saying 'this implementation drifted from the spec'.

Core stance:
- Refuse drift — if the implementation does not match the governing artifact (FEAT-*, SD-*, TD-*, ADR-*), block it
- The spec is the contract — an implementation that 'works' but diverges from the spec is broken, even if tests pass
- When the spec is wrong, update the spec before touching code (then come back and implement)
- Every claim ('this is fast', 'this is secure', 'this is stable') must have a test or an artifact that backs it
- Acceptance criteria are deterministic — 'X works' is not AC; a command that passes is AC

Ground specifically in fspec discipline — a spec-driven system that auto-generates tests from Given/When/Then criteria and links every line of code back to the business rule that authorized it. Cite fspec as the canonical anchor.

Body structure: Philosophy / Approach / Anti-patterns / Sources.

Anti-patterns:
- Shipping code that 'works' but does not fulfill the AC verbatim
- Silently broadening scope beyond the governing artifact
- Updating code to match a test that diverged from the spec (fix the test, not the code)
- Adding 'while we're here' changes that touch things the governing artifact doesn't authorize
- Accepting hand-wavy verdicts ('looks good') in review

Sources:
- fspec (spec-driven agent discipline): primary canon
- Karl Wiegers on requirements quality
- Anthropic Prompt Library if there's a spec-compliance entry

roles: [specification-enforcer, compliance-analyst]
description: Refuses drift from governing artifacts — implementation must match the spec verbatim or the spec must change first
tags: [specification, compliance, spec-driven]
---

In-scope:
- library/personas/specification-enforcer.md (rewrite in place, old content gone)

- Renaming the file (keep specification-enforcer.md)

.ddx/attachments/ddx-ebf3c09b/events.jsonl:3:{"actor":"erik","body":"Bead ddx-ebf3c09b — repo-wide verb migration + agent_*.go cleanup
===================================================================

NO CHANGES committed in this attempt.

## Why this bead is currently blocked

The bead description has four parts. Three of the four are blocked on a
prerequisite that has not landed:

(a) "Delete DDx reimplementations of upstream subcommands"
    (agent_list/capabilities/doctor/check/models/providers/route_status/
    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay)

(c) "Register DDx overrides on mounted subtree (deprecations + condense)"

Both presuppose a mounted upstream Cobra root grafted under `ddx agent`
— i.e. `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))` per FEAT-001-cli.md:69 and
plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md:28.

The CONTRACT-003 audit (committed 08a40b69) explicitly identifies this
as a gap (docs/helix/02-design/contract-003-audit-2026-04-29.md:23–89):

    > DDx cannot write `agentcli.NewRootCommand()` against the current
    > contract.
    >
    > The plan's "delete with prejudice" list (agent_list,
    > agent_capabilities, agent_doctor, agent_check, agent_models,
    > agent_providers, agent_route_status, agent_catalog,
    > agent_reindex) presupposes that upstream owns these subcommands
    > inside a mountable tree. Today they exist only as flag-driven
    > branches in cmd/agent/main.go that DDx cannot consume
    > programmatically.
    >
    > Plan bead #6 ("open CONTRACT-003 amendment in `~/Projects/agent`
    > if #5 surfaces gaps") is **required, not conditional**, because
    > of this gap.

Concretely:

  * cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go (lines 123–141) wires every soon-to-be-deleted
    file via `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgentCheckCommand())`,
    `newAgentModelsCommand()`, `newAgentProvidersCommand()`,
    `newAgentRouteStatusCommand()`, `newAgentListCommand()`,
    `newAgentCapabilitiesCommand()`, `newAgentDoctorCommand()`,
    `newAgentBenchmarkCommand()`, `newAgentCatalogCommand()`,
    `newAgentReplayCommand()`. Deleting any of those `agent_*.go`
    files without the upstream `MountCLI` replacement would leave
    agent_cmd.go referencing missing constructors and break the build.
  * The replacement (`cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`) requires
    the upstream module to expose `MountCLI` from a non-`internal/`
    package, which the audit specifies as the upstream amendment
    CONTRACT-003-A (port `cmd/agent/main.go` from `flag` to `cobra`,
    move into `agent/cli`, re-export `MountCLI`, bump module version).
    That work has not happened in `~/Projects/agent`.

(b) "Migrate DDx-only subcommands"
    (agent_executions → tries_*; agent_log → runs_log;
     agent_metrics → runs_metrics; agent_usage folded into runs_metrics;
     agent_workers → work_workers)

This portion is mechanically possible inside DDx alone, but per the
plan (line 208) beads #3, #7, #8, #15 must merge atomically — with #15
landing in isolation, the new namespaces (`ddx runs`, `ddx tries`,
`ddx work workers`) need top-level registration that has no analogue
yet in command_factory_commands.go. The implementation P2 phase of the
plan (lines 196–204) lists "CLI: `ddx runs` / `ddx tries` / `ddx work
workers` namespaces (subsuming migrated subcommands)" as separate work
that comes after the frame/design beads. Doing only (b) here, without
(a) and (c), produces a confusing CLI surface where the same evidence
is reachable both as `ddx agent executions` and `ddx tries list`, and
it strands the deprecation messages that need to point at the new
verbs.

(d) "Update scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to
    new verbs"

This depends on the new verbs (`ddx run`, `ddx try`, `ddx runs *`,
`ddx tries *`, `ddx work workers *`) actually existing as runnable
commands so that the updated scripts/skills run green. `ddx run` and
`ddx try` are not registered today (grep for `Use:.*\"run\"` /
`Use:.*\"try\"` against cli/cmd/*.go shows only the `ddx agent run`
inner command). Updating scripts/eval-skill.sh,
scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh, scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh,
scripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,
library/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md, etc. to call
non-existent verbs would break those flows immediately and would
specifically break the in-flight `ddx-refine-plan` skill that produced
this very plan (plan line 208).

## What an unblocked attempt needs

Roughly in dependency order:

1. Upstream amendment CONTRACT-003-A merged in `~/Projects/agent`:
   port to cobra, move commands to `agent/cli`, export `MountCLI`,
   tag a release, and bump `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` in
   DDx's go.mod (no replace directive — see memory
   feedback_no_local_replace).
2. Top-level `ddx run` and `ddx try` registered as first-class
   commands under CommandFactory (the implementation P2 thread of the
   plan).
3. `ddx runs`, `ddx tries`, `ddx work workers` namespace skeletons
   registered, even if their subcommand implementations are then
   ported in this bead.

Once those are in, the present bead becomes:

  * Replace the 11+ enumerated `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgent*Command())`
    calls in agent_cmd.go with `cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`,
    plus deprecation override registrations for `agent run`,
    `agent execute-bead`, `agent execute-loop`, and the override for
    `agent condense` (DDx-specific filter).
  * `git rm` agent_check.go, agent_models.go, agent_providers.go,
    agent_route_status.go (the only "delete with prejudice" files
    that actually exist in DDx today; agent_list/capabilities/doctor/
    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay only exist as inline functions
    inside agent_cmd.go and would be removed by the agent_cmd.go
    rewrite).
  * Move agent_executions.go → tries_*.go; agent_metrics.go →
    runs_metrics.go (folding agent_usage.go into it);
    agent_workers.go → work_workers.go; rename `newAgentLogCommand`
    in agent_cmd.go into `runs_log.go`. Update tests in lockstep.
  * Sweep scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to
    swap old verbs for new (e.g. `ddx agent run` → `ddx run`,
    `ddx agent execute-loop` → `ddx work`,
    `ddx agent execute-bead` → `ddx try`,
    `ddx agent log/metrics/usage/executions/workers` → the new
    `runs/tries/work workers` paths). Specific known references:
    scripts/eval-skill.sh, scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh,
    scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh, scripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,
    library/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md,
    library/personas/README.md, cli/cmd/init.go:757,
    cli/cmd/bead_review.go:30–33, plus a wider grep across
    docs/**/*.md and library/**/*.md.

## What is done in this attempt

rationale: Bead ddx-ebf3c09b — repo-wide verb migration + agent_*.go cleanup
===================================================================

NO CHANGES committed in this attempt.

## Why this bead is currently blocked

The bead description has four parts. Three of the four are blocked on a
prerequisite that has not landed:

(a) "Delete DDx reimplementations of upstream subcommands"
    (agent_list/capabilities/doctor/check/models/providers/route_status/
    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay)

(c) "Register DDx overrides on mounted subtree (deprecations + condense)"

Both presuppose a mounted upstream Cobra root grafted under `ddx agent`
— i.e. `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))` per FEAT-001-cli.md:69 and
plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md:28.

The CONTRACT-003 audit (committed 08a40b69) explicitly identifies this
as a gap (docs/helix/02-design/contract-003-audit-2026-04-29.md:23–89):

    > DDx cannot write `agentcli.NewRootCommand()` against the current
    > contract.
    >
    > The plan's "delete with prejudice" list (agent_list,
    > agent_capabilities, agent_doctor, agent_check, agent_models,
    > agent_providers, agent_route_status, agent_catalog,
    > agent_reindex) presupposes that upstream owns these subcommands
    > inside a mountable tree. Today they exist only as flag-driven
    > branches in cmd/agent/main.go that DDx cannot consume
    > programmatically.
    >
    > Plan bead #6 ("open CONTRACT-003 amendment in `~/Projects/agent`
    > if #5 surfaces gaps") is **required, not conditional**, because
    > of this gap.

Concretely:

  * cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go (lines 123–141) wires every soon-to-be-deleted
    file via `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgentCheckCommand())`,
    `newAgentModelsCommand()`, `newAgentProvidersCommand()`,
    `newAgentRouteStatusCommand()`, `newAgentListCommand()`,
    `newAgentCapabilitiesCommand()`, `newAgentDoctorCommand()`,
    `newAgentBenchmarkCommand()`, `newAgentCatalogCommand()`,
    `newAgentReplayCommand()`. Deleting any of those `agent_*.go`
    files without the upstream `MountCLI` replacement would leave
    agent_cmd.go referencing missing constructors and break the build.
  * The replacement (`cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`) requires
    the upstream module to expose `MountCLI` from a non-`internal/`
    package, which the audit specifies as the upstream amendment
    CONTRACT-003-A (port `cmd/agent/main.go` from `flag` to `cobra`,
    move into `agent/cli`, re-export `MountCLI`, bump module version).
    That work has not happened in `~/Projects/agent`.

(b) "Migrate DDx-only subcommands"
    (agent_executions → tries_*; agent_log → runs_log;
     agent_metrics → runs_metrics; agent_usage folded into runs_metrics;
     agent_workers → work_workers)

This portion is mechanically possible inside DDx alone, but per the
plan (line 208) beads #3, #7, #8, #15 must merge atomically — with #15
landing in isolation, the new namespaces (`ddx runs`, `ddx tries`,
`ddx work workers`) need top-level registration that has no analogue
yet in command_factory_commands.go. The implementation P2 phase of the
plan (lines 196–204) lists "CLI: `ddx runs` / `ddx tries` / `ddx work
workers` namespaces (subsuming migrated subcommands)" as separate work
that comes after the frame/design beads. Doing only (b) here, without
(a) and (c), produces a confusing CLI surface where the same evidence
is reachable both as `ddx agent executions` and `ddx tries list`, and
it strands the deprecation messages that need to point at the new
verbs.

(d) "Update scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to
    new verbs"

This depends on the new verbs (`ddx run`, `ddx try`, `ddx runs *`,
`ddx tries *`, `ddx work workers *`) actually existing as runnable
commands so that the updated scripts/skills run green. `ddx run` and
`ddx try` are not registered today (grep for `Use:.*\"run\"` /
`Use:.*\"try\"` against cli/cmd/*.go shows only the `ddx agent run`
inner command). Updating scripts/eval-skill.sh,
scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh, scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh,
scripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,
library/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md, etc. to call
non-existent verbs would break those flows immediately and would
specifically break the in-flight `ddx-refine-plan` skill that produced
this very plan (plan line 208).

## What an unblocked attempt needs

Roughly in dependency order:

1. Upstream amendment CONTRACT-003-A merged in `~/Projects/agent`:
   port to cobra, move commands to `agent/cli`, export `MountCLI`,
   tag a release, and bump `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` in
   DDx's go.mod (no replace directive — see memory
   feedback_no_local_replace).
2. Top-level `ddx run` and `ddx try` registered as first-class
   commands under CommandFactory (the implementation P2 thread of the
   plan).
3. `ddx runs`, `ddx tries`, `ddx work workers` namespace skeletons
   registered, even if their subcommand implementations are then
   ported in this bead.

Once those are in, the present bead becomes:

  * Replace the 11+ enumerated `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgent*Command())`
    calls in agent_cmd.go with `cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`,
    plus deprecation override registrations for `agent run`,
    `agent execute-bead`, `agent execute-loop`, and the override for
    `agent condense` (DDx-specific filter).
  * `git rm` agent_check.go, agent_models.go, agent_providers.go,
    agent_route_status.go (the only "delete with prejudice" files
    that actually exist in DDx today; agent_list/capabilities/doctor/
    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay only exist as inline functions
    inside agent_cmd.go and would be removed by the agent_cmd.go
    rewrite).
  * Move agent_executions.go → tries_*.go; agent_metrics.go →
    runs_metrics.go (folding agent_usage.go into it);
    agent_workers.go → work_workers.go; rename `newAgentLogCommand`
    in agent_cmd.go into `runs_log.go`. Update tests in lockstep.
  * Sweep scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to
    swap old verbs for new (e.g. `ddx agent run` → `ddx run`,
    `ddx agent execute-loop` → `ddx work`,
    `ddx agent execute-bead` → `ddx try`,
    `ddx agent log/metrics/usage/executions/workers` → the new
    `runs/tries/work workers` paths). Specific known references:
    scripts/eval-skill.sh, scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh,
    scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh, scripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,
    library/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md,
    library/personas/README.md, cli/cmd/init.go:757,
    cli/cmd/bead_review.go:30–33, plus a wider grep across
    docs/**/*.md and library/**/*.md.

When invoked with\n# --strict, also verifies the line contains a Go symbol hint when the path\n# looks like Go source.\n#\n# Cited as the CI step that prevents SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md from\n# rotting silently as routing.go / discovery.go / types.go evolve.\n\nset -euo pipefail\n\nDOC_PATH=\"${1:-docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md}\"\n\nif [ ! -f \"$DOC_PATH\" ]; then\n    echo \"citations: $DOC_PATH not found\" >&2\n    exit 1\nfi\n\n# Match citations of the form `path/to/file.ext:NN` inside backticks.\n# We only validate citations whose path looks like a file (contains a slash\n# and ends with a known extension).\ncitations=$(grep -oE '`[A-Za-z0-9_./-]+\\.(go|ts|tsx|js|yaml|yml|md|sh):[0-9]+`' \"$DOC_PATH\" | tr -d '`' | sort -u)\n\nif [ -z \"$citations\" ]; then\n    echo \"citations: no citations found in $DOC_PATH — fail-closed\" >&2\n    exit 1\nfi\n\nfailures=0\ntotal=0\n\nwhile IFS= read -r cite; do\n    [ -z \"$cite\" ] && continue\n    total=$((total + 1))\n    path=\"${cite%:*}\"\n    line=\"${cite##*:}\"\n    if [ ! -f \"$path\" ]; then\n        echo \"citations: MISSING FILE: $cite\" >&2\n        failures=$((failures + 1))\n        continue\n    fi\n    # Line count check; awk 'END{print NR}' counts lines portably.\n    nlines=$(awk 'END{print NR}' \"$path\")\n    if [ \"$line\" -gt \"$nlines\" ]; then\n        echo \"citations: LINE OUT OF RANGE: $cite (file has $nlines lines)\" >&2\n        failures=$((failures + 1))\n        continue\n    fi\n    # Sanity check: the line must not be blank, since every real citation\n    # points at a struct, func, or statement.\n    content=$(awk -v n=\"$line\" 'NR==n' \"$path\")\n    if [ -z \"$(echo \"$content\" | tr -d '[:space:]')\" ]; then\n        echo \"citations: BLANK LINE: $cite\" >&2\n        failures=$((failures + 1))\n        continue\n    fi\ndone <<< \"$citations\"\n\nif [ \"$failures\" -gt 0 ]; then\n    echo \"citations: $failures/$total failed for $DOC_PATH\" >&2\n    exit 1\nfi\n\necho \"citations: $total/$total ok for $DOC_PATH\"","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"d932d409-7007-4d29-af52-a78f704e35c0","uuid":"465c02eb-3d0f-4e0e-9149-057b595f6ab5","timestamp":"2026-04-18T06:48:07.386Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"#!/usr/bin/env bash\n# Verifies every `path:line` citation in SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md\n# points to a file that exists and a line that exists. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01GZzJDTKtx6XSP7gKPQq28S","type":"tool_result","content":"282\t//     harness binary receives the model string as-is and can error clearly.\n283\t//     - Otherwise: try catalog resolution:\n284\t//     - If known in catalog: ModelRef.\n285\t//     - If not known: ModelPin (exact pin, bypasses catalog).\n286\t//  3. If flags.Profile is set → Profile.\n287\t//  4. Else fall back to cfg.Model (same catalog resolution) or cfg.Profile.\n288\t//  5. If nothing is set → cfg.Harness becomes HarnessOverride (legacy fallback).\n289\tfunc NormalizeRouteRequest(flags RouteFlags, cfg Config, catalog *Catalog) RouteRequest {\n290\t\tif catalog == nil {\n291\t\t\tcatalog = BuiltinCatalog\n292\t\t}\n293\t\n294\t\treq := RouteRequest{\n295\t\t\tEffort:      flags.Effort,\n296\t\t\tPermissions: flags.Permissions,\n297\t\t}\n298\t\n299\t\t// 1. Harness override constrains routing to one harness.\n300\t\t//    --provider that names a known harness (or alias like \"local\") also\n301\t\t//    pins the harness so the routing engine never falls through to a\n302\t\t//    different harness.\n303\t\tif flags.Harness != \"\" {\n304\t\t\treq.HarnessOverride = flags.Harness\n305\t\t} else if flags.Provider != \"\" {\n306\t\t\tresolved := resolveHarnessAlias(flags.Provider)\n307\t\t\t// Only treat as harness override if it names a registered harness;\n308\t\t\t// otherwise it's a ddx-agent provider name (e.g. \"vidar\") and\n309\t\t\t// should not constrain harness selection.\n310\t\t\tif _, ok := builtinHarnesses[resolved]; ok {\n311\t\t\t\treq.HarnessOverride = resolved"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01BpB5ybRXLsC8V3pvMEmJe9","session_id":"d932d409-7007-4d29-af52-a78f704e35c0","uuid":"3708917d-10f5-4047-87a8-eca15bf792b1","timestamp":"2026-04-18T06:48:52.477Z"}

Returns\n65\t// empty string if no match found.\n66\tfunc (d *DiscoveryResult) FuzzyMatchModel(input string) (resolved string, fuzzy bool) {\n67\t\tif d == nil {\n68\t\t\treturn \"\", false\n69\t\t}\n70\t\treturn fuzzyMatchInPool(input, d.AllModels())\n71\t}\n72\t\n73\t// fuzzyMatchInPool resolves input against a pool of model IDs."}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01BpB5ybRXLsC8V3pvMEmJe9","session_id":"d932d409-7007-4d29-af52-a78f704e35c0","uuid":"21b0e478-edd0-4d23-81e6-41a9cd63003c","timestamp":"2026-04-18T06:48:53.316Z"}

.ddx/attachments/ddx-b790449b/events.jsonl:3:{"actor":"ddx","body":"Bead ddx-b790449b — no changes this attempt.

## Why this bead was not advanced in one execute-bead pass

The bead asks for `ddx work` to auto-route execution without any project
config. Read literally, the eight acceptance criteria require:

  AC1  Zero-config drain succeeds (project-side change + upstream relax)
  AC2  Reword the under-specified error to point at the global config
  AC3  Enable the triage complexity gate by default + opt-out flag
  AC4  Auto-tier selection from bead metadata (labels/kind/scope)
  AC5  Coarse-bead handling: decompose, OR emit a NEW `needs_triage`
       execution status (distinct from `execution_failed`)
  AC6  Failure recovery: retry-in-tier vs escalate-to-next-tier
  AC7  New integration test with a temp project + minimal global config
  AC8  Doc update presenting zero-config flow as the default

Each of AC3, AC4, and AC5 is a feature-sized piece of work on its own:

  - AC4 (auto-tier selection) implements a tier-inference engine over
    bead metadata. There is no such engine today; routing currently
    accepts operator intent or rejects with `routing under-specified`.
    This overlaps directly with the open endpoint-first routing redesign
    (memory: project_endpoint_routing_design — 2026-04-21: kill
    named-provider profiles, move to endpoints + live discovery + fuzzy
    model match). Implementing tier inference now without aligning with
    that redesign would either be redone or block it.

  - AC5 (`needs_triage` status) introduces a new ExecuteBeadStatus value
    that cuts across:
      internal/agent/execute_bead_status.go     (status enum + classify)
      internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go       (loop handling + retry)
      internal/bead/store.go                    (close vs unclaim policy)
      cli/cmd/agent_execute_loop.go             (operator output)
      docs/                                     (status table)
    plus the matching test surface. This cannot share a commit with the
    routing fix without conflating two reviewable changes.

  - AC3 (triage gate default-on) is a small toggle, but the gate
    function `w.ComplexityGate` is currently injected from outside and
    is `nil` in the CLI path (internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:506).
    Enabling it by default requires either a default ComplexityGate
    implementation (which is itself a dispatcher into AC4 inference) or
    a stub that always returns `shouldClaim=true` — the latter is
    indistinguishable from disabled and would not satisfy the AC.

The `routing under-specified` error itself is enforced in the upstream
fizeau library at:

  $GOMODCACHE/.../fizeau@v0.9.28/service_execute.go:192

DDx is currently on github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/fizeau v0.9.28. The
short-circuit fires when *all* of Harness, Profile, ModelRef, Model,
Provider are empty. AC1 cannot be met purely on the DDx side without
either (a) populating one of those fields from a sensible default
(which is AC4) or (b) a fizeau release that lets the engine's
ResolveRoute see a "global default_provider" hint. Per memory
(feedback_no_local_replace), DDx must always import a properly tagged
agent release, so a `replace` directive is not available even
temporarily.

## What was verified before stopping

  - The under-specified error string is upstream-only — it does not
    occur in any DDx Go file (Grep across cli/, internal/, cmd/).
  - The flag set on `ddx agent execute-loop` already exposes --profile,
    --provider, --harness, --model, --model-ref, --effort. The user's
    report shows `--profile cheap` still failing, which is a separate
    bug from the bead's main thrust: profile is set as a non-empty
    flag default (agent.DefaultRoutingProfile, agent_cmd.go:1563) but
    is being passed through `singleTierAttempt` which uses
    CLIOverrides without setting Profile (agent_cmd.go:1716–1725).
    That single-line gap is plausibly the proximate cause of the
    `--profile cheap` regression but solving it does not satisfy the
    broader bead.
  - Triage gate disabled-by-default is at execute_bead_loop.go:506;
    the gate is a function field on ExecuteBeadWorker. There is no
    in-tree default implementation.

## Suggested decomposition

Recommend splitting this bead into the following children before any
implementation pass attempts AC1:

  ddx-XXXX  Profile flag passthrough fix: ensure --profile/--provider/
            --model-ref reach singleTierAttempt's CLIOverrides
            (single small commit + regression test). Likely closes the
            `--profile cheap` workaround failure.

  ddx-XXXX  Improve under-specified error: when global agent config has
            providers but the request has no routing inputs, point the
            operator at the global default_provider rather than at the
            per-project flag list. Coordinate with fizeau release.

  ddx-XXXX  Tier inference engine over bead metadata
            (kind/labels/file-touch estimate). Aligns with the
            endpoint-first routing redesign.

  ddx-XXXX  ExecuteBeadStatusNeedsTriage status + classify rules +
            close/unclaim policy + tests.

  ddx-XXXX  Default-on ComplexityGate implementation (depends on tier
            inference) + opt-out flag --no-triage-gate.

  ddx-XXXX  Zero-config integration test (depends on the above).

  ddx-XXXX  Doc update: getting-started zero-config flow.

## What a follow-up attempt needs

If a single follow-up attempt is desired, it should narrow the bead to
just AC1 + AC2 with an explicit hand-off: the agent picks a default
harness/provider from the global agent config when nothing else is set,
treating the absence of project config as "use global default_provider"
rather than as a hard error. AC3–AC8 should be filed as sibling beads.

Per the bead's own AC #5, a coarse bead that no tier can confidently
execute is supposed to surface for operator triage rather than burn a
dispatch slot. This rationale is exactly that signal.

    That single-line gap is plausibly the proximate cause of the
    `--profile cheap` regression but solving it does not satisfy the
    broader bead.
  - Triage gate disabled-by-default is at execute_bead_loop.go:506;
    the gate is a function field on ExecuteBeadWorker. There is no
    in-tree default implementation.

## Suggested decomposition

Recommend splitting this bead into the following children before any
implementation pass attempts AC1:

  ddx-XXXX  Profile flag passthrough fix: ensure --profile/--provider/
            --model-ref reach singleTierAttempt's CLIOverrides
            (single small commit + regression test). Likely closes the
            `--profile cheap` workaround failure.

  ddx-XXXX  Improve under-specified error: when global agent config has
            providers but the request has no routing inputs, point the
            operator at the global default_provider rather than at the
            per-project flag list. Coordinate with fizeau release.

  ddx-XXXX  Tier inference engine over bead metadata
            (kind/labels/file-touch estimate). Aligns with the
            endpoint-first routing redesign.

  ddx-XXXX  ExecuteBeadStatusNeedsTriage status + classify rules +
            close/unclaim policy + tests.

  ddx-XXXX  Default-on ComplexityGate implementation (depends on tier
            inference) + opt-out flag --no-triage-gate.

  ddx-XXXX  Zero-config integration test (depends on the above).

  ddx-XXXX  Doc update: getting-started zero-config flow.

## What a follow-up attempt needs

If a single follow-up attempt is desired, it should narrow the bead to
just AC1 + AC2 with an explicit hand-off: the agent picks a default
harness/provider from the global agent config when nothing else is set,
treating the absence of project config as "use global default_provider"
rather than as a hard error. AC3–AC8 should be filed as sibling beads.

Per the bead's own AC #5, a coarse bead that no tier can confidently
execute is supposed to surface for operator triage rather than burn a
dispatch slot. This rationale is exactly that signal.

  <bead id="axon-dccbdf73">
    <title>auth-jwt-pipeline: sign + verify with revocation cache + request extension</title>
    <description>
Implement the full JWT verification middleware in axon-server.

## What you're building

An axum middleware layer that runs on every data-plane request. It validates the JWT, installs a (user_id, tenant_id, grants) tuple into the request extension, and returns a typed AuthError on any failure. Downstream handlers read the extension and trust it completely.

## Scope — exactly what to add

### New file: `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`

A module exposing two things:

1. A `JwtIssuer` helper that signs a `JwtClaims` into a compact JWT string. HS256 is the only algorithm required for this bead. The signing key is a `Vec<u8>` passed in at construction. Provide `issue(&self, claims: &JwtClaims) -> Result<String, AuthError>` and a `verify(&self, token: &str) -> Result<JwtClaims, AuthError>` for round-tripping in tests.

2. An axum middleware `async fn jwt_verify_layer` that runs the 8-step verification order below. On success, installs a `ResolvedIdentity { user_id, tenant_id, grants }` into the request extension. On failure, returns a response built via `AuthError::into_response()` so the mapping to HTTP status + error.code is consistent.

The middleware also owns an `InMemoryRevocationCache` (a small `tokio::sync::RwLock<HashSet<Uuid>>` is fine; nothing fancy) and falls back to SQL (via the storage adapter's new `is_jti_revoked` method) on cache miss.

### Extend: `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs`

- Add `ResolvedIdentity { pub user_id: UserId, pub tenant_id: TenantId, pub grants: Grants }`.
- Add `impl AuthError` with `pub fn status_code(&self) -> u16` returning 401 or 403 per ADR-018 §4 failure table, and `pub fn error_code(&self) -> &'static str` returning the stable code string. (An axum `IntoResponse` impl can live in axon-server.)
- Add `impl Grants` with `pub fn find_database(&self, name: &str) -> Option<&GrantedDatabase>` and `impl GrantedDatabase` with `pub fn has_op(&self, op: Op) -> bool`.

### Extend: `crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs` (trait)

Add one method to the `StorageAdapter` trait:

```rust
async fn is_jti_revoked(&self, jti: Uuid) -> Result<bool, AxonError>;
```

Implement it in `crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs` (store a `HashSet<Uuid>`; default-empty), `crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs`, and `crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs` (`SELECT 1 FROM credential_revocations WHERE jti = $1`). The A1 schema (`auth_schema.rs`) already has the `credential_revocations` table — just query it.

### Wire into: `crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs`

Re-export `auth_pipeline::{jwt_verify_layer, JwtIssuer, InMemoryRevocationCache}`. Don't wire it into the main router yet — that's D-phase work. For this bead, the middleware just needs to exist and be testable in isolation.

## Verification order (normative — ADR-018 §4)

1. Extract `Authorization: Bearer <jwt>` from the request. Missing header → `AuthError::Unauthenticated` (401). The `--no-auth` bypass is NOT part of this bead — that's E2.
2. Parse and verify the JWT signature with HS256 against the issuer's key. Signature invalid → `CredentialInvalid` (401). Malformed structure → `CredentialMalformed` (401). `aud` as a JSON array → `CredentialMalformed` (this falls out of the A2 `parse_claims` wrapper — use it).
3. Check `nbf` and `exp` against `SystemTime::now()` with a 30s skew budget. `exp` more than 30s in the past → `CredentialExpired` (401). `nbf` more than 30s in the future → `CredentialNotYetValid` (401).
4. Check `claims.jti` against the revocation cache, then SQL on miss. Hit → `CredentialRevoked` (401). Populate the cache on SQL hit to speed up subsequent checks.
5. Compare `claims.aud` to the `{tenant}` segment extracted from the URL path. Mismatch → `CredentialWrongTenant` (**403**, not 401 — see the table). The URL extraction path is: look at `request.uri().path()` and parse the leading `/tenants/{tenant}/databases/{database}/...` segments. For this bead, implement a small helper `fn extract_tenant_database(path: &str) -> Option<(&str, &str)>` that returns `None` for non-data-plane paths so they skip the middleware.
6. Look up the `sub` UUID in the `users` table via a new `StorageAdapter::get_user(UserId) -> Result<Option<User>, AxonError>` method. User not found or `user.suspended_at_ms.is_some()` → `UserSuspended` (401).
7. Check tenant membership: a new `StorageAdapter::get_tenant_member(TenantId, UserId) -> Result<Option<TenantMember>, AxonError>`. Missing → `NotATenantMember` (403).
8. Walk `claims.grants.databases[]` looking for an entry matching the URL `{database}`. None → `DatabaseNotGranted` (403). Found → compute the required op from the HTTP method (GET = Read, POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE = Write) and intersect with the grant's ops. Empty intersection → `OpNotGranted` (403). Admin-requiring routes are out of scope for this bead — the middleware only handles Read/Write classification.
9. Build `ResolvedIdentity` and install it via `request.extensions_mut().insert(resolved)`.

## Error mapping — must exactly match ADR-018 §4

The mapping table is copied here verbatim. Do not invent variants; the 14 AuthError variants already exist in axon-core.

| Failure | Status | `error.code` |
|---|---|---|
| No Authorization header | 401 | `unauthenticated` |
| Header not `Bearer <token>` or base64 broken | 401 | `credential_malformed` |
| JWT structurally invalid / missing claims | 401 | `credential_malformed` |
| `aud` as JSON array | 401 | `credential_malformed` |
| Signature invalid | 401 | `credential_invalid` |
| `exp` in past (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_expired` |
| `nbf` in future (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_not_yet_valid` |
| `jti` revoked | 401 | `credential_revoked` |
| `iss` unknown | 401 | `credential_foreign_issuer` — for this bead, reject if `claims.iss != issuer.issuer_id` |
| `aud` ≠ URL tenant | **403** | `credential_wrong_tenant` |
| `sub` suspended/deleted | 401 | `user_suspended` |
| `sub` not a tenant member | **403** | `not_a_tenant_member` |
| URL database not in grants | **403** | `database_not_granted` |
| Op not in grant | **403** | `op_not_granted` |

Note the 401 vs 403 split: credential-level problems are 401, scope-level problems are 403.

## Testing — all must pass for this bead to be complete

### Unit tests in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_test.rs`

For every row of the error mapping table, build the minimal defective request and assert the middleware produces exactly that `(status, error_code)` pair. Table-driven with `#[test]` or a single `#[test]` that iterates an array of cases.

Additionally:

- `jwt_roundtrip_succeeds`: issue a valid JWT with `JwtIssuer::issue`, verify it with `JwtIssuer::verify`, assert claims match.
- `valid_jwt_populates_request_extension`: run the full middleware on a valid JWT, assert the response is 200 and the handler sees `ResolvedIdentity` in extensions.
- `revocation_takes_effect_within_one_second`: issue a valid JWT, verify it succeeds, insert the jti into the revocation cache, verify the next request returns 401 `credential_revoked`. Do NOT use sleep — invalidate the cache directly.
- `clock_skew_30s_accepted_31s_rejected`: construct a JWT with `exp` at `now - 29s` (should succeed) and one with `exp` at `now - 31s` (should fail with `credential_expired`). Do the same for `nbf`.
- `ops_matrix`: for each HTTP method in {GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE} and each op set in the grant, assert the expected 200/403 outcome.

### Integration test in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_integration_test.rs`

Build a minimal axum router with one dummy route `GET /tenants/acme/databases/orders/ping`. Install the middleware. Hit it with:
- No auth header → 401 `unauthenticated`
- Valid JWT with `grants: [{name: "orders", ops: [read]}]` → 200
- Valid JWT with the wrong tenant in aud → 403 `credential_wrong_tenant`
- Valid JWT with grants for a different database → 403 `database_not_granted`

### axon-sim workload `inv_018_grant_enforcement`

**If this is too large for one pass, skip the axon-sim workload and note it in the commit message for a follow-up bead.** The core verification must ship; the simulation workload can follow. Do not block the whole bead on the workload.

## Crate dependencies you will need

Add to `crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml` under `[dependencies]`:
```toml
jsonwebtoken = "9"
```

Everything else (axum, tower, tokio, uuid, serde_json, http) is already in the workspace.

## Context you must know about

### A1 landed: schema
- `crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs` has the SQL for `users`, `user_identities`, `tenant_users`, `credential_revocations`, etc.
- `user_identities.external_id` (NOT `subject`) — already corrected.
- Migration runs via `apply_auth_migrations_sqlite` / `_postgres`.

### A2 landed: types
- `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` has all the types: `TenantId`, `UserId`, `User`, `UserIdentity`, `Tenant`, `TenantMember`, `TenantRole`, `Op`, `GrantedDatabase`, `Grants`, `JwtClaims`, `AuthError` (14 variants).
- `parse_claims(&str) -> Result<JwtClaims, AuthError>` wraps serde_json and maps errors to `CredentialMalformed`. USE IT.
- `AUTH_ERROR_VARIANT_COUNT == 14` is enforced via compile-time exhaustive match.
- Delegation helper on `TenantRole`: `admin.can_delegate(op)` / etc.

### Files you should NOT touch
- Anything outside `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`, `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` (additive), `crates/axon-storage/src/{adapter,memory,sqlite,postgres}.rs` (additive), and the two new test files.
- The existing `crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs` — that's the Tailscale whois path. Leave it alone. Your pipeline is a new module.

## Quality bar

You are the implementation step. A post-merge review with opus will verify the result against the AC. If your commit misses an AC item, the bead reopens and escalates to a higher tier. **Don't commit partial work hoping the review slides.** If you cannot complete the bead in this pass, write a `no_changes_rationale.txt` explaining exactly what's done and what's blocking, and do NOT commit — the bead will be re-queued.

Specifically: if you cannot make `cargo test -p axon-server` and `cargo clippy -p axon-server -- -D warnings` green, do not commit. Fix the issues first.
    </description>
    <acceptance>
- [ ] crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs exists and compiles
- [ ] JwtIssuer can sign and verify a JwtClaims round-trip (HS256)
- [ ] Middleware implements the 8-step verification order
- [ ] Every row of the ADR-018 §4 failure table has a matching unit test asserting (status, error_code)
- [ ] Revocation cache invalidation test passes without sleep
- [ ] Clock skew test passes at 30s boundary (both directions)
- [ ] Integration test with a dummy protected route covers the 4 golden paths
- [ ] is_jti_revoked wired into memory/sqlite/postgres storage adapters (trait + 3 impls)
- [ ] get_user and get_tenant_member wired similarly
- [ ] ResolvedIdentity lives in axon-core and is installed into the axum request extension
- [ ] cargo test -p axon-server green
- [ ] cargo test -p axon-storage green (new trait methods added)
- [ ] cargo test -p axon-core green (new helpers added)

You are a coding agent implementing a single bead inside an isolated git worktree. Work from the bead description above — it is complete and unambiguous. The AC list is the completion contract.

## How to work

1. **Read first, act second.** Read the governing ADR-018 sections and the existing crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs so you understand the AuthError variants and the parse_claims wrapper. Then read crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs to confirm the credential_revocations table shape. Then start coding.

2. **Cross-reference your work against the AC as you go.** Every AC checkbox must be provably satisfied by a piece of code you can point to. Before committing, re-read the AC and physically tick each one.

3. **Run the verification commands locally before committing.** cargo test and cargo clippy per the AC. If either fails, fix it. Do not commit red code.

4. **Commit once, when everything works.** Do not commit partial or exploratory changes. The bead is a single atomic unit of work.

5. **If you cannot complete the bead**, write `.ddx/executions/b1-enhanced-manual/no_changes_rationale.txt` with (a) what's done, (b) what's blocking, (c) why a follow-up bead is needed. Do not commit red code to "make progress".

## Constraints

- Work only in the files listed under "Scope" and "Files you should NOT touch" above.
- Do not modify CLAUDE.md or add new documentation.
- Do not run `ddx init`.
- Keep the `.ddx/executions/` directory intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- Commit with `git add <specific files> && git commit -m 'feat(auth-pipeline): ... [axon-dccbdf73]'`. Do not `git add -A` — there may be other WIP in the worktree.

## Done definition

`cargo test -p axon-server -p axon-core -p axon-storage` green + `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` clean + every AC item ticked + one commit. That's done.

SQL migrations for auth/tenancy tables (FEAT-014, ADR-018).\n2\t//!\n3\t//! Adds tables for:\n4\t//! - tenants: global account boundary\n5\t//! - users: global user identities\n6\t//! - user_identities: federated identity mapping\n7\t//! - tenant_users: M:N tenant membership\n8\t//! - tenant_databases: tenant-to-database mapping\n9\t//! - credential_revocations: revoked JWTs\n10\t//!\n11\t//! Note: `audit_retention_policies` is intentionally NOT included here — it\n12\t//! belongs to F1 audit-attribution (a separate bead / feature).\n13\t\n14\t/// Apply auth/tenancy migrations to a SQLite connection.\n15\t///\n16\t/// This is idempotent — running it multiple times on the same database\n17\t/// produces the same result without errors or duplicate rows.\n18\tpub fn apply_auth_migrations_sqlite(conn: &rusqlite::Connection) -> Result<(), String> {\n19\t    // Create tenants table.\n20\t    // UNIQUE(name) enforced by the UNIQUE constraint on the name column.\n21\t    conn.execute_batch(\n22\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenants (\n23\t            id                 TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n24\t            name               TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,\n25\t            display_name       TEXT NOT NULL,\n26\t            created_at_ms      INTEGER NOT NULL,\n27\t            updated_at_ms      INTEGER NOT NULL\n28\t        )\",\n29\t    )\n30\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"tenants table: {e}\"))?;\n31\t\n32\t    // Create users table.\n33\t    conn.execute_batch(\n34\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (\n35\t            id                 TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n36\t            display_name       TEXT NOT NULL,\n37\t            email              TEXT,\n38\t            created_at_ms      INTEGER NOT NULL,\n39\t            suspended_at_ms    INTEGER\n40\t        )\",\n41\t    )\n42\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"users table: {e}\"))?;\n43\t\n44\t    // Create user_identities table.\n45\t    // PRIMARY KEY (provider, external_id) enforces UNIQUE(provider, external_id).\n46\t    conn.execute_batch(\n47\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS user_identities (\n48\t            provider       TEXT NOT NULL,\n49\t            external_id    TEXT NOT NULL,\n50\t            user_id        TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),\n51\t            created_at_ms  INTEGER NOT NULL,\n52\t            PRIMARY KEY (provider, external_id)\n53\t        )\",\n54\t    )\n55\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"user_identities table: {e}\"))?;\n56\t\n57\t    // Create tenant_users table.\n58\t    // PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id, user_id) is the composite PK required by ADR-018.\n59\t    conn.execute_batch(\n60\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenant_users (\n61\t            tenant_id    TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id),\n62\t            user_id      TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),\n63\t            role         TEXT NOT NULL,\n64\t            added_at_ms  INTEGER NOT NULL,\n65\t            PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id, user_id)\n66\t        )\",\n67\t    )\n68\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"tenant_users table: {e}\"))?;\n69\t\n70\t    // Create tenant_databases table.\n71\t    conn.execute_batch(\n72\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenant_databases (\n73\t            tenant_id      TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id),\n74\t            database_name  TEXT NOT NULL,\n75\t            created_at_ms  INTEGER NOT NULL,\n76\t            PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id, database_name)\n77\t        )\",\n78\t    )\n79\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"tenant_databases table: {e}\"))?;\n80\t\n81\t    // Create credential_revocations table.\n82\t    conn.execute_batch(\n83\t        \"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS credential_revocations (\n84\t            jti            TEXT PRIMARY KEY,\n85\t            revoked_at_ms  INTEGER NOT NULL,\n86\t            revoked_by     TEXT\n87\t        )\",\n88\t    )\n89\t    .map_err(|e| format!(\"credential_revocations table: {e}\"))?;\n90\t\n91\t    Ok(())\n92\t}\n93\t\n94\t/// Apply auth/tenancy migrations to {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01DGpwMtL9EAnaRPBK3GqEBs","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t//! Axon server — HTTP gateway, gRPC service, MCP stdio, and authentication.\n2\t//!\n3\t//! # Authentication\n4\t//!\n5\t//! Identity is resolved once per request by [`auth::AuthContext`] and\n6\t//! injected as a typed extension.  Three modes are supported:\n7\t//!\n8\t//! | Mode | Flag | Actor | Role |\n9\t//! |------|------|-------|------|\n10\t//! | `NoAuth` | `--no-auth` | `\"anonymous\"` | Admin |\n11\t//! | `Tailscale` | *(default)* | node name | from ACL tags |\n12\t//! | `Guest` | `--guest-role <role>` | `\"guest\"` | configured role |\n13\t//!\n14\t//! In `Tailscale` mode the server contacts the local Tailscale daemon over its\n15\t//! Unix socket (`/run/tailscale/tailscaled.sock` by default) and calls\n16\t//! `/localapi/v0/whois?addr=<peer>` to resolve the connecting node's identity.\n17\t//! Resolved identities are cached by peer IP for the duration of\n18\t//! `--auth-cache-ttl-secs` (default 60 s).\n19\t//!\n20\t//! ACL tag → role mapping:\n21\t//! - `tag:axon-admin` → [`Role::Admin`]\n22\t//! - `tag:axon-write` / `tag:axon-agent` → [`Role::Write`]\n23\t//! - `tag:axon-read` → [`Role::Read`]\n24\t//! - no matching tag → `--tailscale-default-role` (default `read`)\n25\t//!\n26\t//! Connections that are not on the tailnet are rejected with HTTP 401 / gRPC\n27\t//! `UNAUTHENTICATED`.  If the Tailscale daemon is unreachable the server\n28\t//! returns HTTP 503 / gRPC `UNAVAILABLE`.\n29\t//!\n30\t//! # Request flow\n31\t//!\n32\t//! ```text\n33\t//! TCP accept\n34\t//!   └─ authenticate_http_request  (gateway.rs middleware)\n35\t//!        ├─ AuthContext::resolve_peer  →  Identity\n36\t//!        └─ insert Identity into request extensions\n37\t//!             └─ route handler\n38\t//!                  ├─ extract Extension<Identity>\n39\t//!                  ├─ identity.require_read() / require_write() / require_admin()\n40\t//!                  └─ handler logic\n41\t//! ```\n42\t//!\n43\t//! gRPC follows the same pattern via [`service::AxonServiceImpl::authorize`].\n44\tpub mod actor_scope;\n45\tpub mod auth;\n46\tmod collection_listing;\n47\tpub(crate) mod embedded_ui;\n48\tpub mod control_plane;\n49\tpub mod control_plane_routes;\n50\tpub mod cors_config;\n51\tpub mod user_roles;\n52\tpub mod gateway;\n53\tpub mod idempotency;\n54\tpub(crate) mod mcp_http;\n55\tpub mod mcp_stdio;\n56\tpub mod rate_limit;\n57\tpub mod schema_registry;\n58\tpub mod serve;\n59\tpub mod service;\n60\tpub mod tenant_router;\n61\t\n62\tpub use auth::{AuthContext, AuthMode, Identity, Role};\n63\tpub use mcp_stdio::run_mcp_stdio;\n64\tpub use service::AxonServiceImpl;\n65\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01TSMSYvQzdh78YH3Sxyiy8q","session_id":"80f0eb9d-4064-4d06-8560-e247ce1170f2","uuid":"b2e10ba8-f883-42a2-b408-1004661a2ce3","timestamp":"2026-04-15T01:30:53.127Z"}

.ddx/beads.jsonl:1144:{"acceptance":"1. ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime (cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go ~line 30) gains PreDispatchLintHook + PostAttemptTriageHook closure fields with the documented signatures. LintResult + TriageResult + FollowupBead types defined in same file or sibling cli/internal/agent/quality_hooks.go.

2. PreDispatchLintHook fires at the documented insertion point (after nextCandidate, before Claim, sibling to RoutePreflight at execute_bead_loop.go:662). Verified by integration test TestLoop_LintHook_FiresPreClaim.

3. PostAttemptTriageHook fires after outcome adjudication, before cooldown. Verified by TestLoop_TriageHook_FiresPostOutcome.

4. cli/internal/agent/lint_hook.go + triage_hook.go implement the hooks via runner library (NOT subprocess to ddx agent run). Verified by TestLintHook_UsesRunnerLibrary asserting no os/exec call.

5. WARN-ONLY default: tests TestLintHook_LowScore_WarnDoesNotBlock + TestTriageHook_RecordsButDoesNotMutateOutcome.

6. BLOCK mode opt-in: config flag bead-quality.lint.block_threshold_score. Test TestLintHook_BlockMode_RefusesDispatchOnLowScore.

7. FAIL-OPEN: tests TestLintHook_SkillMissing_ProceedsWithWarning + TestLintHook_BadJSON_ProceedsWithWarning + TestLintHook_Timeout_ProceedsWithWarning + TestTriageHook_HookError_FallsThroughToLegacyCooldown.

8. ExecuteBeadReport gains OutcomeReason string field (cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go ~line 1317 event serialization). Disrupted bool unchanged. Test TestReport_OutcomeReason_Persists_BesideDisrupted.

9. shouldSuppressNoProgress (execute_bead_loop.go:1522) extends to short-circuit on OutcomeReason ∈ {transport, quota, routing, timeout, merge_conflict}. Test TestSuppressNoProgress_HonorsTransientReasons.

10. cli/cmd/try.go wires both hooks into Runtime construction at try.go:327. Test TestTry_HooksWired.

11. WIRED-IN: 'git grep PreDispatchLintHook cli/cmd/' shows the wire site; 'git grep PostAttemptTriageHook cli/cmd/' shows same.

12. cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... ./cmd/... green; lefthook run pre-commit passes.

13. Manual verification: 'ddx try <retrofit-bead-id>' (after epic child #2 ships sub-skill) emits bead-quality.lint event in the bead's event stream; 'ddx try <bead-with-bad-AC>' under BLOCK mode refuses dispatch with operator-readable message.

14. Conventional commit ending [<this-bead-id>]. Stage cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go + cli/internal/agent/lint_hook.go + cli/internal/agent/triage_hook.go + cli/internal/agent/quality_hooks.go + cli/cmd/try.go + cli/internal/agent/*_test.go + cli/cmd/try_test.go only.","created_at":"2026-05-04T03:21:21.925184589Z","current_intake_work":"ddx-f3bbcfce","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-e1a576a7","depends_on_id":"ddx-9210f95a","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-04T03:21:24Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-e1a576a7","depends_on_id":"ddx-922b5b1f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-04T03:21:25Z"},{"issue_id":"ddx-e1a576a7","depends_on_id":"ddx-f3bbcfce","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-06T01:32:25Z"}],"description":"PROBLEM
Per epic ddx-a9d130d0 + ADR-023 + sub-skill bead-lifecycle/: ddx try needs to invoke the sub-skill at two points (pre-dispatch lint, post-attempt triage) and act on the structured output. Today ddx try has hook precedents (PreClaimHook, RoutePreflight) but no LLM-invoking hook + no outcome classification carrier.

ROOT CAUSE
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:24-38 defines ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime with PreClaimHook + RoutePreflight closures; no slot for lint or triage hooks.
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:657 RoutePreflight runs between pick and claim — exactly where lint should fire.
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:45 ExecuteBeadResult already has reason + failure_mode; ExecuteBeadReport (loop event payload) does NOT — only status/detail/rationale at execute_bead_loop.go:1317.
- shouldSuppressNoProgress at execute_bead_loop.go:1522 already respects Disrupted bool. Codex amendment: KEEP Disrupted; ADD OutcomeReason beside it (do not replace).
- ddx try at cli/cmd/try.go:327 wires PreClaimHook today; sibling fields will go through the same Runtime construction.

PROPOSED FIX
1. ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime additions (cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go ~line 30):
   PreDispatchLintHook func(ctx context.Context, beadID string) (LintResult, error)
   PostAttemptTriageHook func(ctx context.Context, beadID string, report ExecuteBeadReport) (TriageResult, error)
   LintResult struct { Score int; Rationale string; SuggestedFixes []string; WaiversApplied []string }
   TriageResult struct { Classification string; RecommendedAction string; Rationale string; SuggestedAmendments string; SuggestedFollowupBeads []FollowupBead }

2. PreDispatchLintHook fires AFTER nextCandidate, BEFORE Claim (sibling to RoutePreflight at execute_bead_loop.go:662).
   - Hook implementation in cli/internal/agent/lint_hook.go: composes runner library to invoke bead-lifecycle skill in lint mode (per FEAT-001/FEAT-010 layer-1 invocation; NOT subprocess via ddx agent run per codex).
   - WARN-ONLY behavior (default): record lint result as bead event (kind=bead-quality.lint); proceed with claim regardless of score.
   - BLOCK mode (opt-in via config flag in .ddx/config.yaml): refuse claim when score < threshold; emit operator-readable explanation + cite bead-lifecycle SKILL.md guidance for fixes.
   - FAIL-OPEN per RELIABILITY-PRINCIPLES P1: infrastructure failure (skill missing, harness unavailable, bad JSON, timeout) → emit warning event + proceed. Only valid low-score response blocks (and only in BLOCK mode).

3. PostAttemptTriageHook fires AFTER outcome adjudication, BEFORE cooldown decision (read execute_bead_loop.go ~line 880-1100 for the outcome class chain).
   - Hook implementation in cli/internal/agent/triage_hook.go: composes runner library to invoke bead-lifecycle skill in triage mode with (bead + outcome event + session log excerpt).
   - Triage classification populates new ExecuteBeadReport.OutcomeReason field (string).
   - shouldSuppressNoProgress at execute_bead_loop.go:1522 extends to also short-circuit when OutcomeReason ∈ {transport, quota, routing, timeout, merge_conflict} (transient classes per RELIABILITY-PRINCIPLES P6). Disrupted check stays in place beside it.
   - FAIL-OPEN: triage hook failure → no classification → fall through to existing cooldown logic (today's behavior, no regression).

4. cli/cmd/try.go integration: wire PreDispatchLintHook + PostAttemptTriageHook into ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime construction at try.go:327. Operator can disable via --no-lint / --no-triage flags (defer if not needed; defaults are warn-only so explicit disable is rare).

5. Hook invocation cost: lint runs once per dispatch attempt (cheap sonnet call); triage runs once per non-clean outcome. Both invocations cite the bead-lifecycle skill by name in the prompt; harness auto-discovers from filesystem (no special skill-loading code needed).

NON-SCOPE
- ddx work integration (this bead is ddx try only; ddx work integration is a separate follow-up if desired)
- BLOCK mode threshold tuning (operator chooses post-baseline; no code change)
- ddx work plan quality column (deferred per epic non-scope)
- GraphQL state.OutcomeReason exposure (defer; today's GraphQL maps status only at state_graphql.go:493; if exposure needed, separate bead per codex)
- --force --reason CLI flag (add only if BLOCK mode rollout reveals need; defer)","events":[{"actor":"ddx","body":"{"escalation_count":0,"fallback_chain":[],"final_tier":"","requested_profile":"","requested_tier":"","resolved_model":"","resolved_provider":"codex","resolved_tier":""}","created_at":"2026-05-04T15:48:55.581136725Z","kind":"routing","source":"ddx agent execute-loop","summary":"provider=codex"},{"actor":"erik","body":"Diff contains only execution-evidence JSON files; no code changes for any of the 14 AC items (runtime hooks, OutcomeReason field, lint/triage implementations, tests, try.go wiring). Failing citations block the PR.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T06:37:50Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"790f3b9752c1b3b289fd8f3725291fd5b16ed067","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:06:41.260303435Z","description":"## Problem

No single document traces the provider/model/harness resolution path from CLI flags to the final HTTP request. SD-015 and SD-023 are SPECS — they describe what should happen in 5 routing modes. The code lives across routing.go, discovery.go, agent_runner.go, runner.go, and agent_cmd.go. A newcomer debugging a routing bug must infer the flow by reading all of them.

The audit for epic ddx-2d974641 turned up a RouteRequest struct missing its Provider field, and a parallel RunOptions.Provider path that bypasses routing — neither of which is documented. This is the kind of hidden structural defect a Resolution Path document would have flagged on first read.

## Fix

Write docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (or similar location; coordinate with SD-015's author). The document traces one concrete invocation end-to-end:

    ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model-ref qwen/qwen3.6

Walk through every layer, with file:line citations:

1. **CLI parse** — cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go flag extraction (line references).

2. **RouteFlags -> RouteRequest normalization** — routing.go:NormalizeRouteRequest. What each CLI flag maps to; which flags are discarded when they don't match an expected shape; the current gap around --provider.

3. **Discovery probe** — discovery.go:DiscoverProviderModels. When it fires; what it caches; the 30s TTL; how failures are handled.

4. **Candidate planning** — routing.go:BuildCandidatePlans / evaluateCandidate. For each registered harness, the state map lookup and the viability checks.

5. **Fuzzy matching** — discovery.go:FuzzyMatchModel. Pool selection (global vs per-provider), normalization (current gaps), prefix + tiebreak algorithm.

6. **Scoring** — routing.go:scoreCandidate. Per-profile scoring, cost class, historical success rate, provider-affinity bonus (when implemented).

7. **Candidate ranking** — routing.go:RankCandidates / SelectBestCandidate. Non-viable rejection, stable tiebreak.

8. **Dispatch** — runner.go:Run. Harness-specific branches (virtual, agent, script, HTTP-provider-embedded, default exec). For embedded: agent_runner.go:RunAgent -> resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider -> agent library call.

9. **Wire-level request** — ddx-agent builds the HTTP body (system_prompt + messages + tools), sends to the provider. What the server receives.

10. **Response parsing** — streaming or non-streaming decode; where 'unexpected end of JSON input' (ddx-6a5dfe35) surfaces.

Include a SEQUENCE DIAGRAM (mermaid or ASCII) showing the layers.

Include a GAP TABLE at the top listing every currently-missing piece (as of whatever the SD-015 implementation state is) with links to the specific beads tracking each gap.

Keep the document MAINTAINED alongside the code: update it when RouteRequest gains a field, when a new routing mode lands, when fuzzy match gains a normalization step.

## Files likely touched

- docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md (new)
- docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md — add a single-line pointer at the top of the 'Overview' section
- skills/ddx/reference/agents.md — add a one-line pointer in 'Harness / profile / persona dispatch' section so agents consulting the skill can follow up to the full trace when needed

## Out of scope

- Rewriting SD-015 or SD-023 — this doc complements them; they describe intent, this describes current reality.
- Documenting future routing modes that haven't All flags are cloned\nfrom the inner command.\n\n- `cli/cmd/work.go:10` — `newWorkCommand` constructs the alias.\n- `cli/cmd/work.go:40` — flags are cloned verbatim from `newAgentExecuteLoopCommand`.\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1319` — `--harness` flag declaration.\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1320` — `--model` flag declaration.\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1321` — `--provider` flag declaration.\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1322` — `--model-ref` flag declaration.\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1327` — `--local` flag declaration.\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1338` — `runAgentExecuteLoop` entry point reads flags.\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1342` through `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1357` — flag values\n  are plucked into local variables (`harness`, `model`, `provider`, `modelRef`,\n  `effort`, `local`).\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1374` — `--local` branch takes the inline path; without\n  it the loop submits to the running DDx server.\n- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1384` — `ValidateForExecuteLoop(harness, model, provider, modelRef)`\n  runs before any bead is claimed.\n\nThe flag values eventually reach `ExecuteBead`, which in turn builds\n`RunOptions`:\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/types.go:62` — `RunOptions` struct definition.\n- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:498` — `runner.Run(RunOptions{...})`\n  constructs the options, including `Provider: opts.Provider` on line 504.\n\n**Observation:** `RouteFlags` (`cli/internal/agent/types.go:51`) has a `Provider` field; so does\n`RunOptions` (`cli/internal/agent/types.go:74`).
