Note the absence of `Provider`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:303` — `flags.Harness != \"\"` branch assigns\n  `HarnessOverride` directly.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:305` — `flags.Provider != \"\"` branch: the\n  provider flag is **only** consulted as a harness-alias lookup. If\n  `--provider vidar-omlx` does not match any entry in `builtinHarnesses`, the\n  value is silently discarded.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:318` — `flags.Model != \"\"` branch routes the\n  model through `catalog.NormalizeModelRef` to produce either `ModelRef` (known\n  in catalog) or `ModelPin` (exact, bypasses catalog policy).\n- `cli/internal/agent/types.go:337` — `RouteRequest` struct definition. Fields:\n  `Profile`, `ModelRef`, `ModelPin`, `Effort`, `Permissions`, `HarnessOverride`.\n  **No `Provider` field — see Gap #1.**\n\n**Concrete outcome for our invocation:**\n`--provider vidar-omlx` does not name a registered harness, so the provider\nflag is dropped at `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:305`. `--model-ref qwen/qwen3.6` flows through\n`NormalizeModelRef`; since it is not in the shared catalog, it becomes a\n`ModelPin`. The resulting `RouteRequest` carries only\n`{ModelPin: \"qwen/qwen3.6\"}`.\n\n## Layer 3 — Discovery probe\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:117` — `init()` sets the process-wide cache\n  TTL to `30 * time.Second`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:124` — `DiscoverProviderModels(workDir, timeout)` entry.\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:125` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:130` — cache hit returns the cached result if still fresh.\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:141` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:155` — probes every configured provider via `providerstatus.Probe`; silently skips unreachable ones (partial failure is tolerated).\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:162` — cache write under mutex.\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:170` — `InvalidateDiscoveryCache` is the only\n  way to force a re-probe in tests.\n\nDiscovery is invoked on the routing hot-path only when a `ModelPin` is not in\nthe catalog:\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:576` — in `ProbeAndBuildCandidatePlans`, the\n  discovery probe fires when `req.ModelPin != \"\" && req.ModelRef == \"\" && r.Discovery == nil`.\n\nDiscovery is invoked a second time on the dispatch path:\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:521` — in `resolveNativeAgentProvider`,\n  `r.Discovery.ProvidersForModel(model)` looks up which providers advertised\n  the requested model.\n\n## Layer 4 — Candidate planning\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:27` — `BuildCandidatePlans(req, stateOverride)` entry.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:33` through `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:46` — iterates every registered harness. `TestOnly` harnesses (script, virtual) are skipped unless `req.HarnessOverride` names them explicitly — this is the guard that prevents `script` from leaking into standard-tier fallback chains (see ddx-869848ec in the comment on line 40).\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:47` — calls `evaluateCandidate` per harness.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:106` — `evaluateCandidate(name, harness, req, stateOverride)`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:396` — `rejectIfNotViable(plan, req)` runs the\n  viability gate. Checks:\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:399` — `!s.Installed`.\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:402` — `!s.Reachable`.\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:405` — `!s.Authenticated`.\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:408` — quota exhausted.\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:411` — degraded.\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:414` — policy violation.\n  - `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:418` — harness override mismatch.\n\nNote: none of these check *capability* — context window, tool support, effort\nlevel support. That is Gap #6.\n\n## Layer 5 — Fuzzy matching\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:66` — `FuzzyMatchModel(input)` entry.\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:70` — delegates to `fuzzyMatchInPool` using\n  `d.AllModels()` (a flat deduplicated pool across all providers — not per-provider).\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:76` — `fuzzyMatchInPool(input, pool)`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:78` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:82` — exact-match short-circuit.\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:89` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:96` — prefix match: candidate `m` is considered if `len(m) > len(input) && m[:len(input)] == input`. **The comparison is case-sensitive and treats slashes literally (Gap #3).**\n- `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:100` through `cli/internal/agent/discovery.go:106` — tiebreak: shortest suffix wins; alphabetical on equal suffix length. **Not version-aware (Gap #5).**\n\n**Concrete outcome for our invocation:**\nPool likely contains `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit`. Input `qwen/qwen3.6` fails the\nexact check, then fails the prefix check because `Q != q` and the slash is not\nin the pool entries. The function returns `(\"\", false)`. `resolveNativeAgentProvider`\nfalls through to the `isOpenRouterModel` heuristic at\n`cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:544` (vendor/model format → openrouter),\nwhich is not what the operator wanted.\n\n## Layer 6 — Scoring\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:477` — `scoreCandidate(profile, plan)` entry.\n  Returns a `float64`; higher is better.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:478` — base score `100`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:485` — `switch profile` over `cheap`, `standard`, `smart`, default.\n  - `cheap` (lines 486–493): `local` +40, within-quota subscription +20, penalize `cr * 30`.\n  - `standard` (lines 495–502): `local` +25, within-quota +15, penalize `cr * 10`.\n  - `smart` (lines 504–512): `cr * 20` bonus (expensive > cheap), within-quota +5.\n  - default (lines 514–522): treated as standard.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:525` — quota >80%: penalty scales with over-usage.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:528` — quota state unknown: −3.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:531` — routing-signal freshness: `cached` −1, `stale` −4.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:542` — historical success rate (≥3 samples):\n  ≥0.8 +20, <0.5 −30.\n\n**No provider-affinity bonus (Gap #4).** If two harnesses both report viable\nfor the same requested model, `scoreCandidate` does not look at\n`plan.Provider` vs the provider the model was discovered on.\n\n## Layer 7 — Candidate ranking and selection\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:434` — `RankCandidates(profile, plans)`.\n  - Assigns scores (line 444) or `-1` for non-viable (line 441).\n  - Stable sort (lines 448–466): viable before non-viable; descending score;\n    tiebreak prefers local-cost candidates, then alphabetical harness name.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:556` — `SelectBestCandidate(plans)` returns\n  the first viable plan, or `\"no viable harness candidate: all harnesses rejected\"`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/routing.go:567` — `ProbeAndBuildCandidatePlans` is the\n  full-probe wrapper used by actual dispatch (it calls live `ProbeHarnessState`\n  per harness before building plans).\n\n## Layer 8 — Dispatch\n\n`Runner.Run` is the dispatch fork:\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:141` — `Run(opts RunOptions) (*Result, error)` entry.\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:148` — `resolveHarness(opts)` picks the harness.\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:376` — `resolveHarness(opts)` definition.\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:382` — **Gap #2 origin**: if `opts.Harness == \"\"` and\n  `opts.Provider != \"\"`, the `--provider` value is looked up as a harness\n  alias. If it matches a registered harness (e.g. `agent`, `local`), that\n  harness is chosen; otherwise `resolveHarness` falls back to `Config.Harness`.\n  This is a parallel path that bypasses `NormalizeRouteRequest` entirely.\n\nDispatch branches (in order):\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:165` — `harnessName == \"virtual\"` → `RunVirtual(opts)`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:170` — `harnessName == \"agent\"` → `RunAgent(opts)` (embedded).\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:175` — `harnessName == \"script\"` → `RunScript(opts)`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:187` — HTTP-provider harnesses with no binary (`lmstudio`, `openrouter`): surfaces the harness name as `opts.Provider` and delegates to `RunAgent`. The comment on lines 185–186 references `ddx-501e87ef` — the bug where cheap-tier attempts were burning <1s with \"exec: no command\".\n- `cli/internal/agent/runner.go:278` — default exec path: `Executor.ExecuteInDir(ctx, harness.Binary, args, stdin, execDir)`.\n\nEmbedded agent dispatch:\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:65` — `RunAgent(opts)`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:94` — `resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider(wd, model, opts.Provider, opts.ModelRef)`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:464` — `resolveEmbeddedAgentProvider` entry.\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:493` — `resolveNativeAgentProvider(workDir, model, explicitProvider, explicitModelRef)`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:519` — **Gap #2 again**: `explicitProvider != \"\"` overrides the default provider with the raw `opts.Provider` string. This is where `--provider vidar-omlx` finally re-enters the flow, but by now the routing engine has already ranked candidates without knowing about it.\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:521` through `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:548` — discovery-based provider resolution: if the model isn't in the catalog and discovery has results, route to the first provider that advertises the model (exact or fuzzy).\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:555` — `cfg.BuildProviderWithOverrides(providerName, overrides)` materializes the provider object from the native agent config.\n\n## Layer 9 — Wire-level HTTP request\n\nThe embedded agent library builds the request body and dispatches to the\nprovider endpoint. DDx hands off at `agentlib.Run`.\n\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:127` — system prompt construction via\n  `prompt.NewFromPreset(agentCfg.Preset).WithTools(tools).WithContextFiles(...).WithWorkDir(wd).Build()`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:153` — `agentlib.Request` assembly:\n  `Prompt`, `SystemPrompt`, `Provider`, `Tools`, `MaxIterations`, `WorkDir`,\n  `Metadata`, `Compactor`.\n- `cli/internal/agent/agent_runner.go:320` — `agentResult, err := agentlib.Run(ctx, req)`\n  — handoff into the `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` library. When invoked with\n# --strict, also verifies the line contains a Go symbol hint when the path\n# looks like Go source.\n#\n# Cited as the CI step that prevents SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md from\n# rotting silently as routing.go / discovery.go / types.go evolve.\n\nset -euo pipefail\n\nDOC_PATH=\"${1:-docs/helix/02-design/solution-designs/SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md}\"\n\nif [ ! -f \"$DOC_PATH\" ]; then\n    echo \"citations: $DOC_PATH not found\" >&2\n    exit 1\nfi\n\n# Match citations of the form `path/to/file.ext:NN` inside backticks.\n# We only validate citations whose path looks like a file (contains a slash\n# and ends with a known extension).\ncitations=$(grep -oE '`[A-Za-z0-9_./-]+\\.(go|ts|tsx|js|yaml|yml|md|sh):[0-9]+`' \"$DOC_PATH\" | tr -d '`' | sort -u)\n\nif [ -z \"$citations\" ]; then\n    echo \"citations: no citations found in $DOC_PATH — fail-closed\" >&2\n    exit 1\nfi\n\nfailures=0\ntotal=0\n\nwhile IFS= read -r cite; do\n    [ -z \"$cite\" ] && continue\n    total=$((total + 1))\n    path=\"${cite%:*}\"\n    line=\"${cite##*:}\"\n    if [ ! -f \"$path\" ]; then\n        echo \"citations: MISSING FILE: $cite\" >&2\n        failures=$((failures + 1))\n        continue\n    fi\n    # Line count check; awk 'END{print NR}' counts lines portably.\n    nlines=$(awk 'END{print NR}' \"$path\")\n    if [ \"$line\" -gt \"$nlines\" ]; then\n        echo \"citations: LINE OUT OF RANGE: $cite (file has $nlines lines)\" >&2\n        failures=$((failures + 1))\n        continue\n    fi\n    # Sanity check: the line must not be blank, since every real citation\n    # points at a struct, func, or statement.\n    content=$(awk -v n=\"$line\" 'NR==n' \"$path\")\n    if [ -z \"$(echo \"$content\" | tr -d '[:space:]')\" ]; then\n        echo \"citations: BLANK LINE: $cite\" >&2\n        failures=$((failures + 1))\n        continue\n    fi\ndone <<< \"$citations\"\n\nif [ \"$failures\" -gt 0 ]; then\n    echo \"citations: $failures/$total failed for $DOC_PATH\" >&2\n    exit 1\nfi\n\necho \"citations: $total/$total ok for $DOC_PATH\"","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"d932d409-7007-4d29-af52-a78f704e35c0","uuid":"465c02eb-3d0f-4e0e-9149-057b595f6ab5","timestamp":"2026-04-18T06:48:07.386Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"#!/usr/bin/env bash\n# Verifies every `path:line` citation in SD-015-resolution-path-trace.md\n# points to a file that exists and a line that exists. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01GZzJDTKtx6XSP7gKPQq28S","type":"tool_result","content":"282\t//     harness binary receives the model string as-is and can error clearly.\n283\t//     - Otherwise: try catalog resolution:\n284\t//     - If known in catalog: ModelRef.\n285\t//     - If not known: ModelPin (exact pin, bypasses catalog).\n286\t//  3. If flags.Profile is set → Profile.\n287\t//  4. Else fall back to cfg.Model (same catalog resolution) or cfg.Profile.\n288\t//  5. If nothing is set → cfg.Harness becomes HarnessOverride (legacy fallback).\n289\tfunc NormalizeRouteRequest(flags RouteFlags, cfg Config, catalog *Catalog) RouteRequest {\n290\t\tif catalog == nil {\n291\t\t\tcatalog = BuiltinCatalog\n292\t\t}\n293\t\n294\t\treq := RouteRequest{\n295\t\t\tEffort:      flags.Effort,\n296\t\t\tPermissions: flags.Permissions,\n297\t\t}\n298\t\n299\t\t// 1. Harness override constrains routing to one harness.\n300\t\t//    --provider that names a known harness (or alias like \"local\") also\n301\t\t//    pins the harness so the routing engine never falls through to a\n302\t\t//    different harness.\n303\t\tif flags.Harness != \"\" {\n304\t\t\treq.HarnessOverride = flags.Harness\n305\t\t} else if flags.Provider != \"\" {\n306\t\t\tresolved := resolveHarnessAlias(flags.Provider)\n307\t\t\t// Only treat as harness override if it names a registered harness;\n308\t\t\t// otherwise it's a ddx-agent provider name (e.g. \"vidar\") and\n309\t\t\t// should not constrain harness selection.\n310\t\t\tif _, ok := builtinHarnesses[resolved]; ok {\n311\t\t\t\treq.HarnessOverride = resolved"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01BpB5ybRXLsC8V3pvMEmJe9","session_id":"d932d409-7007-4d29-af52-a78f704e35c0","uuid":"3708917d-10f5-4047-87a8-eca15bf792b1","timestamp":"2026-04-18T06:48:52.477Z"}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01FbNtbFVUbDesKpUsBMMxbR","type":"tool_result","content":"470\t\n471\t// scoreCandidate returns a score for a viable candidate given the profile.\n472\t// Higher is better.\n473\t//\n474\t// Routing priority policy:\n475\t//   - cheap/standard: local (free, no quota) > subscription-within-quota > pay-per-token\n476\t//   - smart: cloud capability wins; local models are typically quantized and lower-quality\n477\tfunc scoreCandidate(profile string, plan CandidatePlan) float64 {\n478\t\tbase := 100.0\n479\t\n480\t\tcr := costClassRank[plan.CostClass] // 0=local,1=cheap,2=medium,3=expensive\n481\t\n482\t\t// withinQuota is true when a subscription harness has headroom (cost already sunk).\n483\t\twithinQuota := plan.IsSubscription && (plan.State.QuotaOK || plan.State.QuotaState == \"ok\")\n484\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01BpB5ybRXLsC8V3pvMEmJe9","session_id":"d932d409-7007-4d29-af52-a78f704e35c0","uuid":"93141aca-fb97-4955-80e0-dc1986fc1d6f","timestamp":"2026-04-18T06:48:53.965Z"}

===================================================================

NO CHANGES committed in this attempt.

## Why this bead is currently blocked

The bead description has four parts. Three of the four are blocked on a
prerequisite that has not landed:

(a) "Delete DDx reimplementations of upstream subcommands"
    (agent_list/capabilities/doctor/check/models/providers/route_status/
    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay)

(c) "Register DDx overrides on mounted subtree (deprecations + condense)"

Both presuppose a mounted upstream Cobra root grafted under `ddx agent`
— i.e. `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))` per FEAT-001-cli.md:69 and
plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md:28.

The CONTRACT-003 audit (committed 08a40b69) explicitly identifies this
as a gap (docs/helix/02-design/contract-003-audit-2026-04-29.md:23–89):

    > DDx cannot write `agentcli.NewRootCommand()` against the current
    > contract.
    >
    > The plan's "delete with prejudice" list (agent_list,
    > agent_capabilities, agent_doctor, agent_check, agent_models,
    > agent_providers, agent_route_status, agent_catalog,
    > agent_reindex) presupposes that upstream owns these subcommands
    > inside a mountable tree. Today they exist only as flag-driven
    > branches in cmd/agent/main.go that DDx cannot consume
    > programmatically.
    >
    > Plan bead #6 ("open CONTRACT-003 amendment in `~/Projects/agent`
    > if #5 surfaces gaps") is **required, not conditional**, because
    > of this gap.

Concretely:

  * cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go (lines 123–141) wires every soon-to-be-deleted
    file via `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgentCheckCommand())`,
    `newAgentModelsCommand()`, `newAgentProvidersCommand()`,
    `newAgentRouteStatusCommand()`, `newAgentListCommand()`,
    `newAgentCapabilitiesCommand()`, `newAgentDoctorCommand()`,
    `newAgentBenchmarkCommand()`, `newAgentCatalogCommand()`,
    `newAgentReplayCommand()`. Deleting any of those `agent_*.go`
    files without the upstream `MountCLI` replacement would leave
    agent_cmd.go referencing missing constructors and break the build.
  * The replacement (`cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`) requires
    the upstream module to expose `MountCLI` from a non-`internal/`
    package, which the audit specifies as the upstream amendment
    CONTRACT-003-A (port `cmd/agent/main.go` from `flag` to `cobra`,
    move into `agent/cli`, re-export `MountCLI`, bump module version).
    That work has not happened in `~/Projects/agent`.

(b) "Migrate DDx-only subcommands"
    (agent_executions → tries_*; agent_log → runs_log;
     agent_metrics → runs_metrics; agent_usage folded into runs_metrics;
     agent_workers → work_workers)

This portion is mechanically possible inside DDx alone, but per the
plan (line 208) beads #3, #7, #8, #15 must merge atomically — with #15
landing in isolation, the new namespaces (`ddx runs`, `ddx tries`,
`ddx work workers`) need top-level registration that has no analogue
yet in command_factory_commands.go. The implementation P2 phase of the
plan (lines 196–204) lists "CLI: `ddx runs` / `ddx tries` / `ddx work
workers` namespaces (subsuming migrated subcommands)" as separate work
that comes after the frame/design beads. Doing only (b) here, without
(a) and (c), produces a confusing CLI surface where the same evidence
is reachable both as `ddx agent executions` and `ddx tries list`, and
it strands the deprecation messages that need to point at the new
verbs.

(d) "Update scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to
    new verbs"

This depends on the new verbs (`ddx run`, `ddx try`, `ddx runs *`,
`ddx tries *`, `ddx work workers *`) actually existing as runnable
commands so that the updated scripts/skills run green. `ddx run` and
`ddx try` are not registered today (grep for `Use:.*\"run\"` /
`Use:.*\"try\"` against cli/cmd/*.go shows only the `ddx agent run`
inner command). Updating scripts/eval-skill.sh,
scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh, scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh,
scripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,
library/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md, etc. to call
non-existent verbs would break those flows immediately and would
specifically break the in-flight `ddx-refine-plan` skill that produced
this very plan (plan line 208).

## What an unblocked attempt needs

Roughly in dependency order:

1. Upstream amendment CONTRACT-003-A merged in `~/Projects/agent`:
   port to cobra, move commands to `agent/cli`, export `MountCLI`,
   tag a release, and bump `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` in
   DDx's go.mod (no replace directive — see memory
   feedback_no_local_replace).
2. Top-level `ddx run` and `ddx try` registered as first-class
   commands under CommandFactory (the implementation P2 thread of the
   plan).
3. `ddx runs`, `ddx tries`, `ddx work workers` namespace skeletons
   registered, even if their subcommand implementations are then
   ported in this bead.

Once those are in, the present bead becomes:

  * Replace the 11+ enumerated `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgent*Command())`
    calls in agent_cmd.go with `cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`,
    plus deprecation override registrations for `agent run`,
    `agent execute-bead`, `agent execute-loop`, and the override for
    `agent condense` (DDx-specific filter).
  * `git rm` agent_check.go, agent_models.go, agent_providers.go,
    agent_route_status.go (the only "delete with prejudice" files
    that actually exist in DDx today; agent_list/capabilities/doctor/
    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay only exist as inline functions
    inside agent_cmd.go and would be removed by the agent_cmd.go
    rewrite).
  * Move agent_executions.go → tries_*.go; agent_metrics.go →
    runs_metrics.go (folding agent_usage.go into it);
    agent_workers.go → work_workers.go; rename `newAgentLogCommand`
    in agent_cmd.go into `runs_log.go`. Update tests in lockstep.
  * Sweep scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to
    swap old verbs for new (e.g. `ddx agent run` → `ddx run`,
    `ddx agent execute-loop` → `ddx work`,
    `ddx agent execute-bead` → `ddx try`,
    `ddx agent log/metrics/usage/executions/workers` → the new
    `runs/tries/work workers` paths). Specific known references:
    scripts/eval-skill.sh, scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh,
    scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh, scripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,
    library/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md,
    library/personas/README.md, cli/cmd/init.go:757,
    cli/cmd/bead_review.go:30–33, plus a wider grep across
    docs/**/*.md and library/**/*.md.

## What is done in this attempt

Nothing on disk — but the verification above (audit cross-reference,
agent_cmd.go wiring inspection, FEAT-001 surface inspection,
search for top-level `run`/`try` registrations) is captured here so
the next attempt can move directly into execution once bead #6 lands.

## Recommended next steps

- Park this bead behind bead #6 (upstream CONTRACT-003-A amendment)
  and the P2 implementation beads that introduce top-level
  `ddx run` / `ddx try` and the `runs`/`tries`/`work workers`
  namespace skeletons.
- Re-attempt with the same id once those prerequisites have merged.
  At that point this bead is mechanical: a contained refactor of
  agent_cmd.go plus a directed grep-and-edit sweep of the call sites
  enumerated above.

Bound MCP tool responses and REST JSON responses
that inline library-doc, execution-bundle, diff, session, or persona
content. Add `truncated` and `original_bytes` fields to the response
shapes that FEAT-002 owns.

Implements FEAT-022 requirements §10 and §11 for the non-GraphQL
server surfaces. Satisfies US-224 for the MCP and REST paths.

Depends on Stage A1 (ddx-64299657) only. Parallel with E2.

In-scope files:
- cli/internal/server/server.go (MCP handlers):
  - :3135 mcpReadDocument
  - :3244 diff JSON response
  - :3455 session payloads
  - :3694 persona content
  - All other mcpContent{Type:"text"} literals inlining
    library/execution/diff/session/persona content. Current count ~20+
    sites — enumerate exhaustively in the implementing PR description.
- cli/internal/server/server.go (REST handlers):
  - :937 handleReadDocument
  - :2108 handleAgentWorkerPrompt
  - :2356 handleAgentSessionDetail
- cli/internal/server/state_executions.go (:206 loadExecutionBundleDetail)

Response-shape changes:
- MCP tool response payload: extend the response struct with
  `truncated: bool` and `original_bytes: int` fields (present
  regardless of truncation; false/0 when not truncated).
- REST JSON response shapes for the three handlers: same fields added
  at the document/session/execution detail level.

Coordinate with FEAT-002 on response-shape ownership — FEAT-022
§Dependencies notes the expectation that FEAT-002 carry a reciprocal
mention. Include that cross-reference note in the implementing PR.

Out-of-scope:
- GraphQL schema, resolvers, or generated files (Stage E2).
- Client-side consumption of the new fields (separate UI work).
- Endpoints returning metadata-only data that does not inline the
  five content classes above.

The bead asks for `ddx work` to auto-route execution without any project

Each of AC3, AC4, and AC5 is a feature-sized piece of work on its own:

  - AC4 (auto-tier selection) implements a tier-inference engine over
    bead metadata. There is no such engine today; routing currently
    accepts operator intent or rejects with `routing under-specified`.
    This overlaps directly with the open endpoint-first routing redesign
    (memory: project_endpoint_routing_design — 2026-04-21: kill
    named-provider profiles, move to endpoints + live discovery + fuzzy
    model match). Implementing tier inference now without aligning with
    that redesign would either be redone or block it.

  - AC5 (`needs_triage` status) introduces a new ExecuteBeadStatus value
    that cuts across:
      internal/agent/execute_bead_status.go     (status enum + classify)
      internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go       (loop handling + retry)
      internal/bead/store.go                    (close vs unclaim policy)
      cli/cmd/agent_execute_loop.go             (operator output)
      docs/                                     (status table)
    plus the matching test surface. This cannot share a commit with the
    routing fix without conflating two reviewable changes.

  - AC3 (triage gate default-on) is a small toggle, but the gate
    function `w.ComplexityGate` is currently injected from outside and
    is `nil` in the CLI path (internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go:506).
    Enabling it by default requires either a default ComplexityGate
    implementation (which is itself a dispatcher into AC4 inference) or
    a stub that always returns `shouldClaim=true` — the latter is
    indistinguishable from disabled and would not satisfy the AC.

fizeau library at:

  $GOMODCACHE/.../fizeau@v0.9.28/service_execute.go:192

DDx is currently on github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/fizeau v0.9.28. The
short-circuit fires when *all* of Harness, Profile, ModelRef, Model,
Provider are empty. AC1 cannot be met purely on the DDx side without
either (a) populating one of those fields from a sensible default
(which is AC4) or (b) a fizeau release that lets the engine's
ResolveRoute see a "global default_provider" hint. Per memory
(feedback_no_local_replace), DDx must always import a properly tagged
agent release, so a `replace` directive is not available even
temporarily.

## Why this bead is currently blocked

The bead description has four parts. Three of the four are blocked on a
prerequisite that has not landed:

(a) "Delete DDx reimplementations of upstream subcommands"
    (agent_list/capabilities/doctor/check/models/providers/route_status/
    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay)

(c) "Register DDx overrides on mounted subtree (deprecations + condense)"

Both presuppose a mounted upstream Cobra root grafted under `ddx agent`
— i.e. `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))` per FEAT-001-cli.md:69 and
plan-2026-04-29-artifact-and-run-architecture.md:28.

The CONTRACT-003 audit (committed 08a40b69) explicitly identifies this
as a gap (docs/helix/02-design/contract-003-audit-2026-04-29.md:23–89):

    > DDx cannot write `agentcli.NewRootCommand()` against the current
    > contract.
    >
    > The plan's "delete with prejudice" list (agent_list,
    > agent_capabilities, agent_doctor, agent_check, agent_models,
    > agent_providers, agent_route_status, agent_catalog,
    > agent_reindex) presupposes that upstream owns these subcommands
    > inside a mountable tree. Today they exist only as flag-driven
    > branches in cmd/agent/main.go that DDx cannot consume
    > programmatically.
    >
    > Plan bead #6 ("open CONTRACT-003 amendment in `~/Projects/agent`
    > if #5 surfaces gaps") is **required, not conditional**, because
    > of this gap.

Concretely:

  * cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go (lines 123–141) wires every soon-to-be-deleted
    file via `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgentCheckCommand())`,
    `newAgentModelsCommand()`, `newAgentProvidersCommand()`,
    `newAgentRouteStatusCommand()`, `newAgentListCommand()`,
    `newAgentCapabilitiesCommand()`, `newAgentDoctorCommand()`,
    `newAgentBenchmarkCommand()`, `newAgentCatalogCommand()`,
    `newAgentReplayCommand()`. Deleting any of those `agent_*.go`
    files without the upstream `MountCLI` replacement would leave
    agent_cmd.go referencing missing constructors and break the build.
  * The replacement (`cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`) requires
    the upstream module to expose `MountCLI` from a non-`internal/`
    package, which the audit specifies as the upstream amendment
    CONTRACT-003-A (port `cmd/agent/main.go` from `flag` to `cobra`,
    move into `agent/cli`, re-export `MountCLI`, bump module version).
    That work has not happened in `~/Projects/agent`.

(b) "Migrate DDx-only subcommands"
    (agent_executions → tries_*; agent_log → runs_log;
     agent_metrics → runs_metrics; agent_usage folded into runs_metrics;
     agent_workers → work_workers)

This portion is mechanically possible inside DDx alone, but per the
plan (line 208) beads #3, #7, #8, #15 must merge atomically — with #15
landing in isolation, the new namespaces (`ddx runs`, `ddx tries`,
`ddx work workers`) need top-level registration that has no analogue
yet in command_factory_commands.go. The implementation P2 phase of the
plan (lines 196–204) lists "CLI: `ddx runs` / `ddx tries` / `ddx work
workers` namespaces (subsuming migrated subcommands)" as separate work
that comes after the frame/design beads. Doing only (b) here, without
(a) and (c), produces a confusing CLI surface where the same evidence
is reachable both as `ddx agent executions` and `ddx tries list`, and
it strands the deprecation messages that need to point at the new
verbs.

(d) "Update scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to
    new verbs"

This depends on the new verbs (`ddx run`, `ddx try`, `ddx runs *`,
`ddx tries *`, `ddx work workers *`) actually existing as runnable
commands so that the updated scripts/skills run green. `ddx run` and
`ddx try` are not registered today (grep for `Use:.*\"run\"` /
`Use:.*\"try\"` against cli/cmd/*.go shows only the `ddx agent run`
inner command). Updating scripts/eval-skill.sh,
scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh, scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh,
scripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,
library/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md, etc. to call
non-existent verbs would break those flows immediately and would
specifically break the in-flight `ddx-refine-plan` skill that produced
this very plan (plan line 208).

## What an unblocked attempt needs

Roughly in dependency order:

1. Upstream amendment CONTRACT-003-A merged in `~/Projects/agent`:
   port to cobra, move commands to `agent/cli`, export `MountCLI`,
   tag a release, and bump `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` in
   DDx's go.mod (no replace directive — see memory
   feedback_no_local_replace).
2. Top-level `ddx run` and `ddx try` registered as first-class
   commands under CommandFactory (the implementation P2 thread of the
   plan).
3. `ddx runs`, `ddx tries`, `ddx work workers` namespace skeletons
   registered, even if their subcommand implementations are then
   ported in this bead.

Once those are in, the present bead becomes:

  * Replace the 11+ enumerated `cmd.AddCommand(f.newAgent*Command())`
    calls in agent_cmd.go with `cmd.AddCommand(agent.MountCLI(...))`,
    plus deprecation override registrations for `agent run`,
    `agent execute-bead`, `agent execute-loop`, and the override for
    `agent condense` (DDx-specific filter).
  * `git rm` agent_check.go, agent_models.go, agent_providers.go,
    agent_route_status.go (the only "delete with prejudice" files
    that actually exist in DDx today; agent_list/capabilities/doctor/
    catalog/reindex/benchmark/replay only exist as inline functions
    inside agent_cmd.go and would be removed by the agent_cmd.go
    rewrite).
  * Move agent_executions.go → tries_*.go; agent_metrics.go →
    runs_metrics.go (folding agent_usage.go into it);
    agent_workers.go → work_workers.go; rename `newAgentLogCommand`
    in agent_cmd.go into `runs_log.go`. Update tests in lockstep.
  * Sweep scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to
    swap old verbs for new (e.g. `ddx agent run` → `ddx run`,
    `ddx agent execute-loop` → `ddx work`,
    `ddx agent execute-bead` → `ddx try`,
    `ddx agent log/metrics/usage/executions/workers` → the new
    `runs/tries/work workers` paths). Specific known references:
    scripts/eval-skill.sh, scripts/capture-omlx-fixture.sh,
    scripts/test-doc-snippets.sh, scripts/demos/06-full-journey.sh,
    library/.agents/skills/ddx-refine-plan/SKILL.md,
    library/personas/README.md, cli/cmd/init.go:757,

.ddx/executions/20260425T091407-f52bd996/prompt.md:589:+    "description": "Stage A2 of FEAT-022. Implements the repo-wide lint rule enforcing
the no-unbounded-prompts invariant (FEAT-022 §3 + Non-Functional §Lint).
Satisfies US-226.

Depends on Stage A1 (ddx-64299657) — needs the evidence-package
primitives to exist so the lint rule can whitelist them.

Tooling choice (decided, no worker ambiguity): implement as a Go
analyzer under cli/tools/lint/evidencelint/ runnable via
`go run ./tools/lint/evidencelint/cmd/evidencelint ./...` and wired
into Lefthook pre-commit as an additional check. This is the simplest
path that works with the existing golangci-lint + go-vet stack and
avoids a custom golangci-lint plugin build. If this choice proves
wrong, revisit in a follow-up bead — do not expand scope here.

The analyzer flags, and requires either routing through evidence.* or
an `evidence:allow-unbounded reason="..."` comment on:
- Any assignment expression whose LHS is a selector matching
  `*.Prompt` where the receiver type is the agent RunOptions struct.
- Any composite-literal construction of `mcpContent` with `Type: "text"`.
- Any call to `os.ReadFile` / `ioutil.ReadFile` whose result flows
  into a variable named `*prompt*` or `*Prompt*` (structural heuristic
  covering the identified ingress sites).
- Any HTTP handler function writing response bytes that it obtained
  from library-doc, execution-bundle, diff, session, or persona
  sources (detected structurally by the source function's package
  prefix, not general dataflow).

Static/literal prompt fragments (constant string expressions compiled
into the binary) are exempt.

In-scope files:
- cli/tools/lint/evidencelint/ (new): analyzer.go, analyzer_test.go,
  and cmd/evidencelint/main.go entry point.
- lefthook.yml (existing): add evidence-lint pre-commit hook.
- .github/workflows/ CI config: add evidence-lint job or fold into
  existing lint job.

Out-of-scope:
- Any existing prompt-building call site. This bead only adds the
  analyzer. Existing violations are expected and tolerated via
  `evidence:allow-unbounded` annotations added in subsequent stages
  (B–E) as those stages migrate each site.
- Dataflow analysis. Structural coverage only — per FEAT-022
  Non-Functional §Lint.

Rollback: remove cli/tools/lint/evidencelint/, revert lefthook.yml
and CI workflow edits.",

.ddx/executions/20260423T043852-295d0247/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../personas` is a well-crafted browser + bind UX, but it is read-only: no add, no edit, no delete, no explanation of what the reader is looking at or why they cannot change it. A user looking at it can reasonably ask "what is this for?" and "why can't I change anything?" and the UI answers neither.

## Ground truth

- **What a persona is.** A markdown file with frontmatter under `library/personas/*.md` defining AI personality, expertise, principles, communication style. Shipped defaults: architect, code-reviewer, implementer, specification-enforcer, test-engineer.
- **How it's used.** Projects declare role→persona bindings in `.ddx/config.yaml` via `persona_bindings`. When an agent performs role X, the bound persona's body is injected into its system prompt.
- **What the tool exposes today.**
  - CLI: `ddx persona --list`, `--show <name>`, `--bind <persona> --role <role>`. No create/edit/delete.
  - GraphQL: `personaBind` mutation only (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:150`). No `personaCreate/Update/Delete` in schema.
  - UI: list/show/bind parity with CLI.
- **Why it evolved this way.** Personas have been treated as *library content* — authored carefully, version-controlled upstream, pulled into projects via `ddx update` and contributed back via `ddx contribute`. The tool deliberately did not become a persona editor.

## The gap

The library-only model doesn't hold up for real use:

1. **Project-local personas are a real need.** Teams want "our code-reviewer, with our opinions about our codebase." Today they either (a) fork the persona file and push upstream (wrong — it's not generally useful), (b) never customize (bad outcomes), or (c) drop a file into some ad-hoc local path and hope it works.
2. **The UI implies capability it doesn't have.** A list + detail view with no hint about source policy reads as "unfinished feature" rather than "intentional read-only".
3. **Binding alone isn't enough.** Even if editing stays out of scope, users need to understand *which personas are available*, *where they come from* (library vs project-local), and *how to change that*.

## Proposed direction

Two decisions (pick before implementation):

### D1 — Policy: who owns persona content?

Recommend: **both, with a clear boundary.**
- Library personas: `library/personas/*.md` — read-only in UI, contribute upstream via `ddx contribute`.
- Project-local personas: new directory `.ddx/personas/*.md` — fully editable in UI, live with the project, not in the shared library. Override semantics: a project-local persona with the same name as a library persona takes precedence for that project's bindings.

### D2 — Scope of the UI

Minimum viable, given D1:
- **Explainer copy** on the personas page header: "Personas are AI personality templates. Library personas are shared; project personas live with this project. Bind a persona to a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`."
- **Source badge** on each persona row: `library` or `project`.
- **Project-local editor.** For project personas:
  - `New persona` button → form (name, roles/tags, body markdown). Creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` with frontmatter.
  - `Edit` on project rows.
  - `Delete` on project rows with confirm.
- **Library rows stay read-only.** Action on a library row: `Fork to project` — copies the library file to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` (with a default rename suggestion on collision) and opens the editor. No in-place edit, no delete.
- **Bind affordance remains.** Binding UI continues to work for both sources.

## What this bead does NOT decide

- Multi-project persona sharing (e.g., org-level personas between library and project). File a follow-up if it becomes pressing.
- Rich editor affordances beyond a plain markdown textarea with preview. Frontmatter can be a YAML-ish textarea for v1.
- Syncing project personas upstream — that remains `ddx contribute` and is out of scope.
- Any change to how personas are injected into agent prompts (the read path on the server).

## CLI parity

Whatever the UI gains, the CLI gains too. Add:
- `ddx persona new <name>` — creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` scaffold.
- `ddx persona edit <name>` — opens `$EDITOR` on the project-local persona (errors on library).
- `ddx persona fork <library-name> [--as <new-name>]` — copies library → project.
- `ddx persona delete <name>` — project-local only.

UI and CLI call the same underlying `cli/internal/persona` operations; no duplicate logic.",
.ddx/executions/20260423T043852-295d0247/manifest.json:14:    "acceptance": "**User story:** As a developer looking at the personas UI, I want to understand at a glance what personas are, where they come from (library vs project), and I want to fork or create a project-local persona without leaving the UI. Library content stays read-only so I cannot accidentally mutate shared content; project content is fully editable.

**Acceptance criteria:**

1. **Design note + policy decision recorded.** Before implementation, a one-page note in `docs/helix/02-design/` captures D1 (library vs project policy, override rules, file locations) and D2 (scoped UI additions). Implementation PR links to it.

2. **Project-local persona directory.** `.ddx/personas/*.md` is a recognized location. `ddx persona --list` shows both library and project personas with a `source` tag. Override rule: a project persona with the same name as a library persona wins for that project's bindings; unit test covers this precedence.

3. **GraphQL additions.** New mutations: `personaCreate(name, body, projectId) → Persona`, `personaUpdate(name, body, projectId) → Persona`, `personaDelete(name, projectId) → {ok}`. All gated on `source == "project"`; attempting any of them on a library persona returns a typed error. Each mutation persists to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` under the given project.

4. **CLI parity.** `ddx persona new`, `edit`, `fork`, `delete` exist and share the same underlying operations as the GraphQL mutations (no duplicated logic). Integration test asserts that a `ddx persona new` followed by a GraphQL `personas` query returns the new persona.

5. **UI — explainer copy.** The personas page shows a one-sentence description of what personas are and a second sentence distinguishing library vs project sources. Playwright asserts both sentences are present.

6. **UI — source badge.** Each persona row shows a `library` or `project` badge. Playwright seeds one of each and asserts correct badges.

7. **UI — New / Edit / Delete for project personas.** A "New persona" button opens a form with name + body fields. Project-source rows have Edit and Delete affordances. Library-source rows have neither. Playwright creates a persona, edits it, verifies the edit persisted, then deletes it.

8. **UI — Fork library to project.** Library rows have a "Fork to project" action. Clicking it prompts for a name (default: library name with `-local` suffix on collision), creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md`, and navigates to the editor. Playwright asserts the forked file exists and opens in the editor.

9. **No regressions.** Existing list/show/bind flows still work. Playwright smoke of the pre-existing binding UX passes.

10. **Empty project-local state.** When a project has zero project-local personas, the UI shows a subtle hint: "No project personas yet. Fork a library persona or create a new one." Playwright asserts.",

- Any change to how personas are injected into agent prompts (the read path on the server).

## CLI parity

Whatever the UI gains, the CLI gains too. Add:
- `ddx persona new <name>` — creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` scaffold.
- `ddx persona edit <name>` — opens `$EDITOR` on the project-local persona (errors on library).
- `ddx persona fork <library-name> [--as <new-name>]` — copies library → project.
- `ddx persona delete <name>` — project-local only.

UI and CLI call the same underlying `cli/internal/persona` operations; no duplicate logic.

.ddx/executions/20260423T042946-811098c4/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../personas` is a well-crafted browser + bind UX, but it is read-only: no add, no edit, no delete, no explanation of what the reader is looking at or why they cannot change it. A user looking at it can reasonably ask "what is this for?" and "why can't I change anything?" and the UI answers neither.

## Ground truth

- **What a persona is.** A markdown file with frontmatter under `library/personas/*.md` defining AI personality, expertise, principles, communication style. Shipped defaults: architect, code-reviewer, implementer, specification-enforcer, test-engineer.
- **How it's used.** Projects declare role→persona bindings in `.ddx/config.yaml` via `persona_bindings`. When an agent performs role X, the bound persona's body is injected into its system prompt.
- **What the tool exposes today.**
  - CLI: `ddx persona --list`, `--show <name>`, `--bind <persona> --role <role>`. No create/edit/delete.
  - GraphQL: `personaBind` mutation only (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:150`). No `personaCreate/Update/Delete` in schema.
  - UI: list/show/bind parity with CLI.
- **Why it evolved this way.** Personas have been treated as *library content* — authored carefully, version-controlled upstream, pulled into projects via `ddx update` and contributed back via `ddx contribute`. The tool deliberately did not become a persona editor.

## The gap

The library-only model doesn't hold up for real use:

1. **Project-local personas are a real need.** Teams want "our code-reviewer, with our opinions about our codebase." Today they either (a) fork the persona file and push upstream (wrong — it's not generally useful), (b) never customize (bad outcomes), or (c) drop a file into some ad-hoc local path and hope it works.
2. **The UI implies capability it doesn't have.** A list + detail view with no hint about source policy reads as "unfinished feature" rather than "intentional read-only".
3. **Binding alone isn't enough.** Even if editing stays out of scope, users need to understand *which personas are available*, *where they come from* (library vs project-local), and *how to change that*.

## Proposed direction

Two decisions (pick before implementation):

### D1 — Policy: who owns persona content?

Recommend: **both, with a clear boundary.**
- Library personas: `library/personas/*.md` — read-only in UI, contribute upstream via `ddx contribute`.
- Project-local personas: new directory `.ddx/personas/*.md` — fully editable in UI, live with the project, not in the shared library. Override semantics: a project-local persona with the same name as a library persona takes precedence for that project's bindings.

### D2 — Scope of the UI

Minimum viable, given D1:
- **Explainer copy** on the personas page header: "Personas are AI personality templates. Library personas are shared; project personas live with this project. Bind a persona to a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`."
- **Source badge** on each persona row: `library` or `project`.
- **Project-local editor.** For project personas:
  - `New persona` button → form (name, roles/tags, body markdown). Creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` with frontmatter.
  - `Edit` on project rows.
  - `Delete` on project rows with confirm.
- **Library rows stay read-only.** Action on a library row: `Fork to project` — copies the library file to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` (with a default rename suggestion on collision) and opens the editor. No in-place edit, no delete.
- **Bind affordance remains.** Binding UI continues to work for both sources.

## What this bead does NOT decide

- Multi-project persona sharing (e.g., org-level personas between library and project). File a follow-up if it becomes pressing.
- Rich editor affordances beyond a plain markdown textarea with preview. Frontmatter can be a YAML-ish textarea for v1.
- Syncing project personas upstream — that remains `ddx contribute` and is out of scope.
- Any change to how personas are injected into agent prompts (the read path on the server).

## CLI parity

Whatever the UI gains, the CLI gains too. Add:
- `ddx persona new <name>` — creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` scaffold.
- `ddx persona edit <name>` — opens `$EDITOR` on the project-local persona (errors on library).
- `ddx persona fork <library-name> [--as <new-name>]` — copies library → project.
- `ddx persona delete <name>` — project-local only.

UI and CLI call the same underlying `cli/internal/persona` operations; no duplicate logic.",

.ddx/executions/20260423T031959-e177fccf/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../personas` is a well-crafted browser + bind UX, but it is read-only: no add, no edit, no delete, no explanation of what the reader is looking at or why they cannot change it. A user looking at it can reasonably ask "what is this for?" and "why can't I change anything?" and the UI answers neither.

## Ground truth

- **What a persona is.** A markdown file with frontmatter under `library/personas/*.md` defining AI personality, expertise, principles, communication style. Shipped defaults: architect, code-reviewer, implementer, specification-enforcer, test-engineer.
- **How it's used.** Projects declare role→persona bindings in `.ddx/config.yaml` via `persona_bindings`. When an agent performs role X, the bound persona's body is injected into its system prompt.
- **What the tool exposes today.**
  - CLI: `ddx persona --list`, `--show <name>`, `--bind <persona> --role <role>`. No create/edit/delete.
  - GraphQL: `personaBind` mutation only (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:150`). No `personaCreate/Update/Delete` in schema.
  - UI: list/show/bind parity with CLI.
- **Why it evolved this way.** Personas have been treated as *library content* — authored carefully, version-controlled upstream, pulled into projects via `ddx update` and contributed back via `ddx contribute`. The tool deliberately did not become a persona editor.

## The gap

The library-only model doesn't hold up for real use:

1. **Project-local personas are a real need.** Teams want "our code-reviewer, with our opinions about our codebase." Today they either (a) fork the persona file and push upstream (wrong — it's not generally useful), (b) never customize (bad outcomes), or (c) drop a file into some ad-hoc local path and hope it works.
2. **The UI implies capability it doesn't have.** A list + detail view with no hint about source policy reads as "unfinished feature" rather than "intentional read-only".
3. **Binding alone isn't enough.** Even if editing stays out of scope, users need to understand *which personas are available*, *where they come from* (library vs project-local), and *how to change that*.

## Proposed direction

Two decisions (pick before implementation):

### D1 — Policy: who owns persona content?

Recommend: **both, with a clear boundary.**
- Library personas: `library/personas/*.md` — read-only in UI, contribute upstream via `ddx contribute`.
- Project-local personas: new directory `.ddx/personas/*.md` — fully editable in UI, live with the project, not in the shared library. Override semantics: a project-local persona with the same name as a library persona takes precedence for that project's bindings.

### D2 — Scope of the UI

Minimum viable, given D1:
- **Explainer copy** on the personas page header: "Personas are AI personality templates. Library personas are shared; project personas live with this project. Bind a persona to a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`."
- **Source badge** on each persona row: `library` or `project`.
- **Project-local editor.** For project personas:
  - `New persona` button → form (name, roles/tags, body markdown). Creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` with frontmatter.
  - `Edit` on project rows.
  - `Delete` on project rows with confirm.
- **Library rows stay read-only.** Action on a library row: `Fork to project` — copies the library file to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` (with a default rename suggestion on collision) and opens the editor. No in-place edit, no delete.
- **Bind affordance remains.** Binding UI continues to work for both sources.

## What this bead does NOT decide

- Multi-project persona sharing (e.g., org-level personas between library and project). File a follow-up if it becomes pressing.
- Rich editor affordances beyond a plain markdown textarea with preview. Frontmatter can be a YAML-ish textarea for v1.
- Syncing project personas upstream — that remains `ddx contribute` and is out of scope.
- Any change to how personas are injected into agent prompts (the read path on the server).

## CLI parity

Whatever the UI gains, the CLI gains too. Add:
- `ddx persona new <name>` — creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` scaffold.
- `ddx persona edit <name>` — opens `$EDITOR` on the project-local persona (errors on library).
- `ddx persona fork <library-name> [--as <new-name>]` — copies library → project.
- `ddx persona delete <name>` — project-local only.

UI and CLI call the same underlying `cli/internal/persona` operations; no duplicate logic.",

10. **Empty project-local state.** When a project has zero project-local personas, the UI shows a subtle hint: "No project personas yet. Fork a library persona or create a new one." Playwright asserts.",

.ddx/executions/20260423T023822-a2d45fb0/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../personas` is a well-crafted browser + bind UX, but it is read-only: no add, no edit, no delete, no explanation of what the reader is looking at or why they cannot change it. A user looking at it can reasonably ask "what is this for?" and "why can't I change anything?" and the UI answers neither.

## Ground truth

- **What a persona is.** A markdown file with frontmatter under `library/personas/*.md` defining AI personality, expertise, principles, communication style. Shipped defaults: architect, code-reviewer, implementer, specification-enforcer, test-engineer.
- **How it's used.** Projects declare role→persona bindings in `.ddx/config.yaml` via `persona_bindings`. When an agent performs role X, the bound persona's body is injected into its system prompt.
- **What the tool exposes today.**
  - CLI: `ddx persona --list`, `--show <name>`, `--bind <persona> --role <role>`. No create/edit/delete.
  - GraphQL: `personaBind` mutation only (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:150`). No `personaCreate/Update/Delete` in schema.
  - UI: list/show/bind parity with CLI.
- **Why it evolved this way.** Personas have been treated as *library content* — authored carefully, version-controlled upstream, pulled into projects via `ddx update` and contributed back via `ddx contribute`. The tool deliberately did not become a persona editor.

## The gap

The library-only model doesn't hold up for real use:

1. **Project-local personas are a real need.** Teams want "our code-reviewer, with our opinions about our codebase." Today they either (a) fork the persona file and push upstream (wrong — it's not generally useful), (b) never customize (bad outcomes), or (c) drop a file into some ad-hoc local path and hope it works.
2. **The UI implies capability it doesn't have.** A list + detail view with no hint about source policy reads as "unfinished feature" rather than "intentional read-only".
3. **Binding alone isn't enough.** Even if editing stays out of scope, users need to understand *which personas are available*, *where they come from* (library vs project-local), and *how to change that*.

## Proposed direction

Two decisions (pick before implementation):

### D1 — Policy: who owns persona content?

Recommend: **both, with a clear boundary.**
- Library personas: `library/personas/*.md` — read-only in UI, contribute upstream via `ddx contribute`.
- Project-local personas: new directory `.ddx/personas/*.md` — fully editable in UI, live with the project, not in the shared library. Override semantics: a project-local persona with the same name as a library persona takes precedence for that project's bindings.

### D2 — Scope of the UI

Minimum viable, given D1:
- **Explainer copy** on the personas page header: "Personas are AI personality templates. Library personas are shared; project personas live with this project. Bind a persona to a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`."
- **Source badge** on each persona row: `library` or `project`.
- **Project-local editor.** For project personas:
  - `New persona` button → form (name, roles/tags, body markdown). Creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` with frontmatter.
  - `Edit` on project rows.
  - `Delete` on project rows with confirm.
- **Library rows stay read-only.** Action on a library row: `Fork to project` — copies the library file to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` (with a default rename suggestion on collision) and opens the editor. No in-place edit, no delete.
- **Bind affordance remains.** Binding UI continues to work for both sources.

## What this bead does NOT decide

- Multi-project persona sharing (e.g., org-level personas between library and project). File a follow-up if it becomes pressing.
- Rich editor affordances beyond a plain markdown textarea with preview. Frontmatter can be a YAML-ish textarea for v1.
- Syncing project personas upstream — that remains `ddx contribute` and is out of scope.
- Any change to how personas are injected into agent prompts (the read path on the server).

## CLI parity

Whatever the UI gains, the CLI gains too. Add:
- `ddx persona new <name>` — creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` scaffold.
- `ddx persona edit <name>` — opens `$EDITOR` on the project-local persona (errors on library).
- `ddx persona fork <library-name> [--as <new-name>]` — copies library → project.
- `ddx persona delete <name>` — project-local only.

UI and CLI call the same underlying `cli/internal/persona` operations; no duplicate logic.",

.ddx/executions/20260422T210036-1b58752f/prompt.md:5:## Observed

`/nodes/.../projects/.../personas` is a well-crafted browser + bind UX, but it is read-only: no add, no edit, no delete, no explanation of what the reader is looking at or why they cannot change it. A user looking at it can reasonably ask "what is this for?" and "why can't I change anything?" and the UI answers neither.

## Ground truth

- **What a persona is.** A markdown file with frontmatter under `library/personas/*.md` defining AI personality, expertise, principles, communication style. Shipped defaults: architect, code-reviewer, implementer, specification-enforcer, test-engineer.
- **How it's used.** Projects declare role→persona bindings in `.ddx/config.yaml` via `persona_bindings`. When an agent performs role X, the bound persona's body is injected into its system prompt.
- **What the tool exposes today.**
  - CLI: `ddx persona --list`, `--show <name>`, `--bind <persona> --role <role>`. No create/edit/delete.
  - GraphQL: `personaBind` mutation only (`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_feat008.go:150`). No `personaCreate/Update/Delete` in schema.
  - UI: list/show/bind parity with CLI.
- **Why it evolved this way.** Personas have been treated as *library content* — authored carefully, version-controlled upstream, pulled into projects via `ddx update` and contributed back via `ddx contribute`. The tool deliberately did not become a persona editor.

## The gap

The library-only model doesn't hold up for real use:

1. **Project-local personas are a real need.** Teams want "our code-reviewer, with our opinions about our codebase." Today they either (a) fork the persona file and push upstream (wrong — it's not generally useful), (b) never customize (bad outcomes), or (c) drop a file into some ad-hoc local path and hope it works.
2. **The UI implies capability it doesn't have.** A list + detail view with no hint about source policy reads as "unfinished feature" rather than "intentional read-only".
3. **Binding alone isn't enough.** Even if editing stays out of scope, users need to understand *which personas are available*, *where they come from* (library vs project-local), and *how to change that*.

## Proposed direction

Two decisions (pick before implementation):

### D1 — Policy: who owns persona content?

Recommend: **both, with a clear boundary.**
- Library personas: `library/personas/*.md` — read-only in UI, contribute upstream via `ddx contribute`.
- Project-local personas: new directory `.ddx/personas/*.md` — fully editable in UI, live with the project, not in the shared library. Override semantics: a project-local persona with the same name as a library persona takes precedence for that project's bindings.

### D2 — Scope of the UI

Minimum viable, given D1:
- **Explainer copy** on the personas page header: "Personas are AI personality templates. Library personas are shared; project personas live with this project. Bind a persona to a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`."
- **Source badge** on each persona row: `library` or `project`.
- **Project-local editor.** For project personas:
  - `New persona` button → form (name, roles/tags, body markdown). Creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` with frontmatter.
  - `Edit` on project rows.
  - `Delete` on project rows with confirm.
- **Library rows stay read-only.** Action on a library row: `Fork to project` — copies the library file to `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` (with a default rename suggestion on collision) and opens the editor. No in-place edit, no delete.
- **Bind affordance remains.** Binding UI continues to work for both sources.

## What this bead does NOT decide

- Multi-project persona sharing (e.g., org-level personas between library and project). File a follow-up if it becomes pressing.
- Rich editor affordances beyond a plain markdown textarea with preview. Frontmatter can be a YAML-ish textarea for v1.
- Syncing project personas upstream — that remains `ddx contribute` and is out of scope.
- Any change to how personas are injected into agent prompts (the read path on the server).

## CLI parity

Whatever the UI gains, the CLI gains too. Add:
- `ddx persona new <name>` — creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md` scaffold.
- `ddx persona edit <name>` — opens `$EDITOR` on the project-local persona (errors on library).
- `ddx persona fork <library-name> [--as <new-name>]` — copies library → project.
- `ddx persona delete <name>` — project-local only.

UI and CLI call the same underlying `cli/internal/persona` operations; no duplicate logic.

5. **UI — explainer copy.** The personas page shows a one-sentence description of what personas are and a second sentence distinguishing library vs project sources. Playwright asserts both sentences are present.

6. **UI — source badge.** Each persona row shows a `library` or `project` badge. Playwright seeds one of each and asserts correct badges.

7. **UI — New / Edit / Delete for project personas.** A "New persona" button opens a form with name + body fields. Project-source rows have Edit and Delete affordances. Library-source rows have neither. Playwright creates a persona, edits it, verifies the edit persisted, then deletes it.

8. **UI — Fork library to project.** Library rows have a "Fork to project" action. Clicking it prompts for a name (default: library name with `-local` suffix on collision), creates `.ddx/personas/<name>.md`, and navigates to the editor. Playwright asserts the forked file exists and opens in the editor.

9. **No regressions.** Existing list/show/bind flows still work. Playwright smoke of the pre-existing binding UX passes.

10. **Empty project-local state.** When a project has zero project-local personas, the UI shows a subtle hint: "No project personas yet. Fork a library persona or create a new one." Playwright asserts.
